Through the window, she could see a figure moving between the shelves. A woman, maybe in her forties, carrying a stack of books.

Mara knocked lightly on the glass.

The woman looked up, startled, then walked over and opened the door.

“We’re closed,” she said automatically, then paused. “Oh. Sorry. I didn’t expect anyone.”

“I was just walking by,” Mara said. “I saw the light.”

The woman nodded. “I’m doing inventory. It’s easier at night.”

Mara glanced inside. “Do you ever get tired of it?”

“Books?” the woman asked.

“Counting them. Organizing them. Making sure everything is where it’s supposed to be.”

The woman smiled faintly. “That’s the thing,” she said. “They’re never all where they’re supposed to be.”

“Isn’t that the point?” Mara said. “To put them back?”

“Sometimes,” the woman said. “But sometimes the interesting part is where they end up instead.”

Mara leaned against the doorframe. “That sounds like a good way to lose things.”

“It is,” the woman agreed. “But it’s also a good way to find things you weren’t looking for.”

Mara considered that.

“Do you want to come in?” the woman asked.

“I don’t think I’m allowed to,” Mara said.

The woman shrugged. “Rules are mostly about timing. Right now, it’s just me.”

Mara hesitated, then stepped inside.

The library smelled faintly of paper and dust, a scent she had always found comforting. The overhead lights were dimmer than usual, casting soft shadows between the shelves.

“I’m Mara,” she said.

“Lena,” the woman replied.

Mara watched as Lena placed a few books onto a cart. “Do you ever feel like everything is just… misplaced?” she asked.

Lena glanced at her. “In what sense?”

“In every sense,” Mara said. “Like things aren’t where they’re supposed to be. Or maybe they are, and you’re the one who’s out of place.”

Lena considered this for a moment.

“I think,” she said slowly, “that we spend a lot of time assuming there’s a ‘supposed to be’ at all.”

Mara frowned. “There isn’t?”

“There are patterns,” Lena said. “Habits. Expectations. But ‘supposed to’ is something we add on top of that. It’s a story we tell ourselves to make things feel more stable.”

“And if the story doesn’t fit?” Mara asked.

“Then you either change the story,” Lena said, “or you accept that it was never quite true to begin with.”

Mara let that settle.

“Do you like your story?” Lena asked.

Mara opened her mouth, then stopped.

“I don’t know,” she admitted.

“That’s a good place to start,” Lena said.

Mara glanced around the library one more time, then stepped back toward the door.

“I should keep going,” she said.

Lena nodded. “Of course. The route doesn’t—”

“Walk itself,” Mara finished, a small smile forming.

Lena raised an eyebrow. “Exactly.”

Mara stepped back out into the night.

By the time she reached the river, the sky had begun to lighten slightly at the edges, a faint gray hinting at the coming dawn. The water moved steadily, reflecting the dim glow of the streetlights.

She leaned against the railing and looked out at the current.

The night had been strange. Unusually so. Three encounters, each one leaving behind a different kind of echo in her mind.

The man in the alley, with his quiet certainty that nothing ever really stopped.

The girl in the park, urging her to go higher.

The woman in the library, gently dismantling the idea of “supposed to.”

Mara exhaled slowly.

Maybe none of it had been real. Maybe she had imagined it all, her mind weaving together fragments of thought into something that felt external.

Or maybe it didn’t matter.

The city was waking up now. A car passed behind her, its tires hissing softly on the pavement. Somewhere in the distance, a door slammed.

Mara straightened and pushed off from the railing.

For the first time in a while, she didn’t feel like she was walking to escape her thoughts.

She was just walking.

And for now, that was enough.
:::writing

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
The night the river forgot its name, no one noticed at first.

It began with a small and reasonable mistake. A sign at the old bridge—white paint, green letters, a respectable municipal font—was replaced after a storm snapped the post. The new sign read simply: RIVER. Not the Brightwell, as it had been called for generations, but just RIVER, as if it had always been that way, as if specificity were a luxury the water could no longer afford.

At first, people laughed. “Budget cuts,” they said. “Or a bored intern.” The fisherman with the red cap shrugged and cast his line beneath the sign. The schoolchildren crossed the bridge in twos and threes, dragging backpacks and the gravity of small lives, not noticing the difference at all. The river ran as it always had, sliding under the bridge, carrying leaves, bottles, secrets, the soft insistence of time.

But names are strange things. They are not only labels but agreements—quiet, invisible treaties between people and the world. And when a treaty dissolves, even something as patient as a river can begin to behave differently.

By the end of the week, the maps changed.

At the town library, Mrs. Halvorsen, who had spent thirty-seven years reshelving histories and cataloging the fragile memory of paper, opened an atlas to show a boy where the Brightwell flowed. She found a blank thread of blue. No name. The boy leaned over the page, his finger hovering.

“What’s it called?” he asked.

Mrs. Halvorsen frowned, not because she didn’t know, but because she suddenly couldn’t prove it. “It’s…” she began, and stopped. The word felt slippery, like trying to catch a fish with bare hands.

“It’s the river,” she said finally, and felt, for the first time in her career, that a book had failed her.

Across town, Mr. Kline, who had lived in the same house for sixty-two years, woke with a peculiar unease. He stood at his kitchen sink, looking out at the ribbon of water beyond his yard, and tried to summon its name the way one summons an old friend’s face. It hovered just out of reach. Bright-something. Bell-something. He turned the syllables over in his mind until they lost their shape entirely.

He went outside and stood at the bank.

“You’re the Bright—” he began, and then laughed, embarrassed, though no one was there. “You’re the river,” he corrected himself.

The river said nothing, but its current seemed to hesitate, as if considering.

By the second week, the changes became harder to ignore.

Boats that had always drifted predictably began to veer. Fishermen complained that the usual spots yielded nothing, while unexpected eddies teemed with life. The water seemed, at times, to run faster than it should, then slow to a contemplative crawl, as if unsure of its destination.

At night, people reported hearing sounds they couldn’t quite describe—soft murmurs, like a crowd speaking just below the threshold of comprehension. Some said it was the wind. Others said it was the water remembering something it had misplaced.

Children, who are more attuned to these shifts, began to play a new game. They would stand on the bridge and call out names—any names they could think of.

“Silverway!”

“Moonrun!”

“Grandmother’s Ribbon!”

Each time, they would watch the water, waiting for a sign of recognition. Sometimes the surface would ripple in a peculiar pattern, as if the river were trying on the name, seeing how it fit.

“Did you see that?” one child would shout.

“It liked that one,” another would insist.

The adults dismissed it as imagination, but more and more of them found themselves pausing on the bridge, listening, as if the river might introduce itself if given enough quiet.

In the third week, the first disappearance occurred.

It was not a person, not in the way tragedies are usually counted. It was the old willow tree that leaned over the eastern bank, its branches trailing like long, green hair in the water. The tree had been there longer than anyone could remember. It appeared in photographs from decades past, a constant presence, a landmark by which directions were given and stories were anchored.

“Turn left at the willow by the Brightwell,” people used to say.

But now there was no Brightwell, and one morning, there was no willow.

In its place stood an empty stretch of bank, as if the tree had never existed. The ground was smooth, undisturbed, no roots torn from the earth, no fallen trunk. Just absence.

At the town hall, a meeting was called.

People gathered in folding chairs, murmuring, their voices carrying the low hum of unease that had settled over the town. On the stage, Mayor Jensen adjusted his tie and cleared his throat.

“I’m sure there’s a reasonable explanation,” he began. “Erosion, perhaps. Or—”

“Trees don’t just vanish,” someone called from the back.

“They do if they were never there,” another voice said, half-joking, half-afraid.

Mrs. Halvorsen stood up, her hands trembling slightly. “We’re forgetting things,” she said. “Not just the river’s name. Other things. Small things, at first. But they’re adding up.”

“What kind of things?” the mayor asked.

She hesitated. “I can’t remember,” she admitted, and a ripple of uneasy laughter moved through the room.

Mr. Kline raised his hand. “The willow,” he said. “I remember it. But when I try to describe it, it feels… thin. Like a story I only half heard.”

A young woman in the front row spoke up. “My grandmother used to tell me that names keep things anchored,” she said. “She said if you forget the name of a place, it starts to drift.”

“That’s superstition,” the mayor replied, though his voice lacked conviction.

“Is it?” she asked.

Silence settled over the room, heavy and unresolved.

That night, the river rose.

Not in a flood, not in any way that would trigger alarms or emergency broadcasts. It simply swelled, quietly, steadily, until it lapped at the edges of the town in places it had never reached before. It seeped into gardens, touched the foundations of houses, pressed its cool presence against the edges of human order.

People woke to find the river closer than it should be.

“It’s just the rain,” some said, though there had been no rain.

“It’ll recede,” others insisted, though it did not.

In the days that followed, more things went missing.

A bench in the park where couples had carved their initials. A narrow footpath that wound through the reeds. A small, unremarkable shed behind the grocery store. Each absence was subtle, almost plausible in isolation, but together they formed a pattern that no one could comfortably ignore.

And always, the river.

It moved with a growing sense of intention, as if it were exploring the boundaries of its existence, testing what it could claim in the absence of a name.

The children’s game became more serious.

They began to keep notebooks, recording the names they tried and the river’s responses. Patterns emerged, though they were difficult to articulate. Some names seemed to calm the water, smoothing its surface into a glassy stillness. Others stirred it into restless motion.

One name, whispered by a quiet girl named Lila, had a particularly striking effect.

“Brightwell,” she said, almost shyly, as if unsure whether she was allowed to say it.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the river stilled.

Not just the surface, but the entire body of water seemed to pause, as if listening. The murmuring sound that people had begun to notice at night fell silent. Even the air felt suspended, caught between breaths.

“Did you feel that?” Lila asked.

The other children nodded, wide-eyed.

“Say it again,” one of them urged.

“Brightwell,” she repeated.

This time, the river responded with a gentle, almost imperceptible current, flowing as it had before, familiar and steady.

Word spread quickly.

By evening, people were gathering on the bridge, drawn by a mixture of curiosity and a fragile, rekindled hope. Lila stood at the center, clutching her notebook, suddenly the focal point of a town’s attention.

“Go on,” someone said. “Say it.”

She swallowed, then spoke clearly. “Brightwell.”

The effect was undeniable.

The river seemed to settle into itself, as if a tension it had been carrying was finally released. The water’s erratic movements smoothed into a recognizable rhythm. The strange murmurs faded, replaced by the familiar sound of flowing water.

“It worked,” someone whispered.

“Of course it did,” Mrs. Halvorsen said softly, though tears had begun to gather in her eyes. “That’s its name.”

The crowd began to murmur the word, tentatively at first, then with growing confidence.

“Brightwell.”

“Brightwell.”

“Brightwell.”

Each repetition seemed to strengthen something invisible but essential, like reinforcing the threads of a fraying fabric.

But not everything returned.

The willow tree did not reappear. The missing bench, the vanished path, the absent shed—these remained gone, as if the restoration of the river’s name could not fully reverse what had already been unmade.

In the following days, the river held steady.

The water receded to its usual boundaries. Boats drifted predictably once more. Fishermen found their familiar spots yielding their usual catch. The town began to exhale, slowly, cautiously, as if unsure whether it was safe to trust this newfound stability.

Life resumed its rhythms, but with a subtle shift.

People spoke the river’s name more often than before, weaving it into conversation with a deliberate care that had not been necessary when it was taken for granted.

“I’ll meet you by the Brightwell.”

“The Brightwell looks calm today.”

“Remember when the Brightwell froze over that winter?”

The name became a touchstone, a shared affirmation of something nearly lost.

Mrs. Halvorsen updated the library’s records, carefully annotating the atlases where the name had vanished, writing “Brightwell” in a steady hand. She began a new project, too—a collection of names, local and otherwise, with notes on their origins and meanings.

“Just in case,” she told anyone who asked.

Mr. Kline took to walking along the river each morning, greeting it quietly.

“Good morning, Brightwell,” he would say, feeling faintly foolish, but also comforted by the ritual.

Lila continued her observations, expanding her notebook into a kind of informal study of names and their effects. She discovered that it wasn’t only the river that responded. Other places, too—the park, the streets, even the town itself—seemed to shift subtly when their names were spoken with intention.

One afternoon, she sat with Mrs. Halvorsen in the library, flipping through old records.

“Why do names matter so much?” Lila asked.

Mrs. Halvorsen considered the question. “I think,” she said slowly, “that names are how we agree on what is real. Not in the sense that things don’t exist without them, but… without a name, it’s harder to hold something in the mind. It slips. It blurs.”

“So the river was slipping?” Lila asked.

“Perhaps,” Mrs. Halvorsen said. “Or perhaps we were letting go of it, without realizing.”

Lila frowned. “But we didn’t choose to forget.”

“No,” Mrs. Halvorsen agreed. “But we didn’t choose to remember, either. Not until we had to.”

That evening, Lila returned to the bridge.

The sun was setting, casting long shadows across the water, turning the surface into a shifting mosaic of gold and shadow. The river flowed steadily beneath her, as it had before, as it would, she hoped, for a long time to come.

“Brightwell,” she said softly.

The river flowed on, unchanged, which was, in itself, a kind of answer.

She opened her notebook and wrote a new entry:

“The river is called Brightwell. It responds to its name. When unnamed, it becomes unstable, unpredictable, and begins to encroach on other things. Naming restores balance, but does not fully undo what has been lost.”

She paused, then added:

“Conclusion: Names are not just labels. They are anchors.”

As she closed the notebook, she noticed something at the edge of the water.

A small sapling, its thin branches bending slightly in the current, its leaves a delicate green. It stood where the old willow had once been, not the same, not yet anything like it, but present.

Lila smiled.

“Hello,” she said, and after a moment’s thought, added, “Willow.”

The leaves trembled, whether from the breeze or something else, she could not say.

Behind her, the town moved in its ordinary ways—people cooking dinner, children finishing homework, televisions flickering to life. The small, steady routines of human existence, each one tied, in its own way, to the names that made them coherent.

The river flowed, named and known.

And somewhere, in the quiet spaces between words, the world held together a little more firmly, as if reminded of itself.
:::writing

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
The city had a way of remembering things people tried to forget. Not in any mystical sense—no whispers in the brickwork, no spectral echoes drifting through alleyways—but in the stubborn, material way that cities hold on to the marks of use. Scratches on subway windows, gum pressed into the sidewalk like fossilized impatience, a half-peeled poster advertising a concert that had happened three summers ago. Memory, here, was sedimentary. Layer after layer, year after year, nothing ever fully erased.

Mara liked that about it. Or at least she told herself she did.

Every morning she took the same train from the outer edge of the city to the center, where the buildings grew taller and the air grew thinner with ambition. She stood in the same place on the platform, just to the left of a pillar streaked with rust. The train doors, when they opened, would align almost perfectly with her spot. It wasn’t exact—nothing ever was—but it was close enough to give the illusion of control.

She worked in an office that specialized in digitizing old records. Birth certificates, census logs, handwritten letters from archives no one visited anymore. Her job was to scan, label, and upload. She spent her days turning paper into data, reducing ink and emotion into searchable fields.

Name. Date. Location.

Sometimes there were notes scribbled in margins. Sometimes a letter would include a pressed flower, long since browned and fragile. These weren’t part of the official record. They were removed, cataloged separately, or discarded if deemed irrelevant.

Mara had learned not to linger on those details. At first, she had. She’d paused over a looping cursive signature, wondering about the hand that had shaped it. She’d read fragments of letters, glimpsing arguments, confessions, small domestic moments preserved by accident. But curiosity slowed her down, and the work was measured in volume. Efficiency mattered.

So she trained herself to look past the human parts.

Still, sometimes something slipped through.

One Tuesday, midway through a stack of documents from the early 1900s, she found a photograph tucked between two census pages. It wasn’t labeled. The image showed a group of people standing in front of a house. The photo had faded unevenly, so that the edges were ghostly pale while the center remained darker, more defined. There were six figures. Five stood in a line, and one—perhaps a child—sat on the steps.

Mara almost removed it without looking closely. It wasn’t part of the assigned material. But something about the way the figures were arranged made her pause. They weren’t smiling. Not in the stiff, formal way people often did in old photographs, but not smiling at all. Their expressions were neutral, almost guarded.

Except for the child.

The child was turned slightly away from the camera, looking off to the side. It was as if something outside the frame had caught their attention at the moment the photo was taken.

Mara leaned closer to the screen. The scan resolution was high enough to capture small details, and she zoomed in on the child’s face. The image pixelated slightly, but she could still make out the direction of the gaze.

There was nothing there. Just the blank edge of the photograph.

She sat back in her chair, suddenly aware of the hum of the fluorescent lights overhead. It was a small thing, meaningless. People looked away from cameras all the time. But the longer she stared at the image, the more it felt like the child was looking at something that had been removed.

Or something that had never been captured in the first place.

She checked the back of the photograph. No writing. No date. No names.

For a moment, she considered slipping it into her bag. It would be easy. No one tracked individual items that closely, especially ones that weren’t part of the official inventory. It would become one more unrecorded thing in a city full of them.

Instead, she placed it in the miscellaneous tray and continued her work.

That night, on the train home, Mara found herself thinking about the photograph again. She tried to reconstruct the scene in her mind, to imagine what might have been just beyond the frame. Another person? A pet? A passing carriage?

Or nothing at all.

The train lurched, and the lights flickered briefly. Across from her, a man in a gray coat was staring at his reflection in the window, his face superimposed over the dark tunnel rushing past. For a moment, Mara thought of the photograph again—the way the child’s gaze seemed to rest on something unseen.

She shook her head, as if to dislodge the thought.

By the time she reached her stop, she had convinced herself it was nothing.

The next day, the photograph was gone.

Mara noticed its absence immediately. The miscellaneous tray where she had placed it was empty, save for a few paperclips and a torn envelope. She checked the surrounding stacks, then the digital log. There was no record of the photograph being processed.

She asked her supervisor, a man named Elliot who always seemed mildly surprised to be addressed.

“Did you move something from my station yesterday?” she asked.

He blinked at her. “What kind of something?”

“A photograph. It wasn’t part of the main set.”

Elliot shook his head. “If it wasn’t logged, it probably got discarded. We don’t keep unverified materials.”

“Without scanning it?”

He shrugged. “Happens. We’re not an archive. We’re a service.”

Mara nodded, though something about the answer felt incomplete. Discarded. Just like that. A small, quiet erasure.

She returned to her desk, but the rhythm of her work felt off. Each document she scanned seemed thinner somehow, less substantial. She found herself glancing at the empty tray, as if expecting the photograph to reappear.

It didn’t.

Days passed. Then weeks.

The city continued its steady accumulation of marks and memories. New posters layered over old ones. Construction sites rose and fell. The train ran on schedule, mostly. Mara settled back into her routine, the photograph fading to the edges of her thoughts.

Until she saw the house.

It was on a street she didn’t usually take, a detour caused by a closed subway line. She walked instead, following a route suggested by her phone. The neighborhood was quieter than her own, the buildings lower, the sidewalks less crowded.

And then there it was.

A house set back from the street, with a narrow set of steps leading up to the front door. The paint was peeling, and the windows were clouded with dust. It might have gone unnoticed, just another aging structure in a city full of them.

Except Mara recognized it immediately.

The photograph had been taken here.

She slowed her pace, her eyes tracing the details. The angle of the steps, the shape of the doorway, even the pattern of cracks in the facade—it all matched. Time had altered the surface, but the underlying structure was unmistakable.

She stopped in front of the house, her heart beating faster than she could justify.

This was coincidence, she told herself. The city had countless houses. Similar designs, repeated across neighborhoods. It wasn’t impossible that she had seen something like this before, that her mind was filling in the gaps.

But the certainty lingered.

Without fully deciding to, she walked up the steps.

The door was closed. Of course it was. She stood there for a moment, unsure of what she expected. There was no sign of life inside. No movement behind the dusty windows.

She raised her hand and knocked.

The sound echoed more than it should have, as if the interior were emptier than the exterior suggested.

No answer.

Mara waited, then knocked again.

Still nothing.

She exhaled, a mix of relief and disappointment, and turned to leave.

“Hello?”

The voice came from behind her.

Mara turned. An older woman stood on the sidewalk, watching her. She held a grocery bag in one hand, her expression curious but not unfriendly.

“Sorry,” Mara said. “I was just—”

“Looking for someone?” the woman asked.

Mara hesitated. “I’m not sure. Do you know who lives here?”

The woman glanced at the house, then back at Mara. “No one, as far as I know. It’s been empty for years.”

“How many years?”

The woman shrugged. “Ten? Maybe more. Time blurs.”

Mara nodded. “Do you know anything about who used to live here?”

The woman shifted the grocery bag to her other hand. “Families. Different ones, over time. I couldn’t tell you names.”

Mara almost let it go. There was no reason to press further. The house was just a house.

But the image of the photograph rose in her mind again—the six figures, the child looking away.

“Was there ever a family with a child who…” She trailed off, unsure how to finish the question.

The woman tilted her head. “A child who what?”

Mara shook her head. “Never mind. It’s nothing.”

The woman studied her for a moment, then nodded. “If you’re curious, you could check the records. City hall keeps that sort of thing.”

Mara almost laughed. Records. She spent her days immersed in them, and yet this—this felt different. Less structured. Less certain.

“Yeah,” she said. “Maybe I will.”

She walked away, but the sense of unfinished business followed her.

That evening, instead of going straight home, Mara stopped at a small public records office near her apartment. It wasn’t part of her job, and the system was older, less streamlined. The air smelled faintly of paper and dust.

She approached the desk, where a clerk sat reading a paperback novel.

“Can I help you?” the clerk asked, marking her place with a finger.

“I’m looking for information about a property,” Mara said, giving the address of the house.

The clerk nodded and gestured to a row of computers. “You can search the database there. If you need older records, we’ve got physical archives in the back.”

Mara thanked her and sat down at one of the terminals.

The database was slow, the interface clunky. She entered the address and waited as the system processed the request.

A list of names appeared. Owners, tenants, dates of occupancy. The house had changed hands several times over the decades, as the woman had said.

Mara clicked through the entries, scanning for anything that might stand out.

Most of it was mundane. Transfers of ownership, rental agreements, minor renovations.

Then she found a record from over a century ago.

A family of six.

The names were listed in a simple column. Two adults, four children.

Mara felt a small, involuntary shiver.

She clicked on the entry for more details, but the information was sparse. Just dates, basic identifiers. No photographs. No descriptions.

She leaned back in her chair, staring at the screen.

Six figures.

She glanced around the room, as if expecting someone to be watching her. The clerk was still at the desk, absorbed in her book.

Mara turned back to the computer.

There was one more link associated with the record. A note indicating that additional materials were available in the physical archives.

She stood and walked to the back.

The archive room was dimmer, the shelves packed tightly with boxes and folders. A small desk sat in the corner, with a sign-in sheet and a pair of cotton gloves.

Mara wrote her name and the reference number from the database, then located the corresponding box.

Inside were a handful of documents. Deeds, correspondence, a few official forms.

And a photograph.

Her breath caught.

It was the same image she had seen at work. The same six figures in front of the house.

But this time, there was something different.

Mara set the box on the desk and carefully lifted the photograph. She brought it closer, her eyes scanning the details.

The five standing figures were the same—unchanged, their expressions neutral.

But the child on the steps—

The child was looking directly at the camera.

Mara froze.

She was certain. Completely, unshakably certain that this was not how the photograph had appeared before. She remembered the angle of the head, the direction of the gaze. She had studied it, zoomed in on it.

The child had been looking away.

Now, the child’s eyes met hers.

A strange, disorienting feeling settled over her, as if the ground beneath her had shifted slightly out of alignment.

She checked the back of the photograph.

There was writing this time.

A single line, in faded ink.

“We thought we lost one.”

Mara stared at the words, her mind struggling to make sense of them.

Lost one.

She looked back at the image.

Six figures.

Had there always been six?

A flicker of doubt crept in. Memory was unreliable. She knew that. She worked with records precisely because they offered a kind of stability that memory could not.

But this—

She closed her eyes, trying to recall the original image. The arrangement, the expressions.

Five standing.

One sitting.

Six total.

That hadn’t changed.

So what had?

Her gaze returned to the child.

The child who was now looking at her.

Mara felt a sudden, irrational urge to put the photograph back and leave. To step out of the archive, out of the building, back into the familiar noise of the city where things made sense in their accumulation of small, tangible details.

Instead, she reached into the box and pulled out the rest of the documents.

If something had changed, she needed to understand how.

She sorted through the papers, scanning for anything unusual.

Most were routine. Legal language, signatures, dates.

Then she found a letter.

It was handwritten, the ink slightly smudged in places. The paper was thin, brittle with age.

Mara unfolded it carefully and began to read.

The letter described a series of small, unsettling occurrences in the house. Objects misplaced, then found in different locations. Doors left open that had been closed. The sense of being watched, even when alone.

At first, the writer dismissed these as forgetfulness, or the natural noises of an old building.

But over time, the tone shifted.

There were references to hearing footsteps at night, to seeing movement in the corners of rooms.

And then, near the end, a line that made Mara’s chest tighten.

“We have begun to notice discrepancies in our own recollections. Small things at first, but growing. It is as if the house itself resists being fully known.”

Mara lowered the letter, her mind racing.

Discrepancies.

She thought of the photograph. Of the child’s shifting gaze.

Of her own certainty, now eroding at the edges.

A sound behind her made her turn.

The archive room was still empty.

But for a moment—just a moment—she had the distinct impression that someone had been standing just beyond her field of vision.

She stood slowly, the letter still in her hand.

The city remembered things, she had always believed. It held onto them in its surfaces, its structures.

But what if it didn’t just remember?

What if it revised?

Mara looked down at the photograph one more time.

The child’s eyes seemed almost alive now, as if aware of being observed.

She felt a strange, unsettling thought take root.

Not that the photograph had changed.

But that she had.

That something in her perception, her memory, had shifted just enough to reveal a different version of the same reality.

She placed the photograph back in the box, her movements deliberate.

Then she signed out of the archive and stepped back into the street.

The city was as it had always been. Crowded, noisy, layered with the traces of countless lives.

But as Mara walked, she found herself noticing things she hadn’t before.

A doorway that seemed slightly narrower than it should be.

A mural with figures arranged in a way that felt subtly off.

A reflection in a window that lagged a fraction of a second behind her movement.

Small things.

Discrepancies.

She stopped at a crosswalk, waiting for the light to change.

Across the street, a group of people stood in front of a building, talking.

For a brief, flickering instant, Mara had the uncanny sense that there were too many of them. That the number didn’t quite add up.

She blinked, and the feeling vanished.

The light turned green.

Mara crossed the street, her mind caught between skepticism and unease.

The city moved around her, indifferent.

And somewhere, beneath the layers of memory and matter, something continued to shift—quietly, persistently—just out of reach of certainty.

Mara kept walking, no longer entirely sure whether she was moving through the city as she had known it, or through some subtle revision of it.

Either way, she had the distinct feeling that whatever had changed was not finished.

And that, whether she liked it or not, she was now part of the record.
:::writing

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
There’s a particular hour of the evening that feels like it belongs to no one. It isn’t quite sunset and not yet night, a thin stretch of time where the light softens and edges blur, as if the world itself is deciding how sharply it wants to exist. If you’re outside when it happens, you can feel it, even if you can’t quite name it—a loosening. Colors fade a little, sounds carry differently, and people seem to move with a slightly altered purpose, like actors who have forgotten their lines but keep walking anyway.

I like that hour. It feels honest.

During the day, everything is too defined. Responsibilities line up like objects on a desk: clear, labeled, and occasionally oppressive. You know what things are. You know what you’re supposed to do. There’s very little room for ambiguity, and even less room for the kind of wandering thoughts that don’t lead anywhere useful. Daytime is practical, efficient, structured. It rewards answers, not questions.

Night, on the other hand, is almost too forgiving. Once darkness settles in fully, things disappear. You can’t see as far, and because of that, the world shrinks. It becomes easier to ignore what’s beyond your immediate surroundings. There’s comfort in that, but also a kind of quiet avoidance. Night lets you retreat. It doesn’t ask much of you.

But that in-between hour—just before night fully takes over—that’s where things get interesting. That’s where questions live.

It’s the hour when you start thinking about things you didn’t have time to think about earlier. Conversations replay themselves. Decisions begin to feel less certain. You notice details you missed before: the way a building catches the last bit of sunlight, the uneven rhythm of footsteps behind you, the hum of something mechanical that you never noticed during the noise of the day.

There’s a kind of honesty in noticing.

I think people underestimate how much of their lives are shaped by what they fail to notice. Not big things, necessarily. It’s rarely the dramatic, obvious events that quietly redirect the course of things. It’s the small, almost invisible moments—the ones that don’t seem important enough to record, but accumulate anyway.

A missed message. A glance that lingers a second too long or not long enough. A decision made out of convenience rather than intention. These things don’t announce themselves. They don’t feel significant in the moment. But they build, layer by layer, until you wake up one day in a life that feels both familiar and slightly foreign.

And then you wonder when it happened.

That question—when did this happen—is more complicated than it seems. It assumes there was a single moment, a turning point you could point to if you looked closely enough. But most of the time, there isn’t. Change is rarely dramatic. It doesn’t arrive with a clear before and after. It seeps in, gradual and persistent, until the “before” feels like something you might have imagined.

Memory doesn’t help much either. It reshapes things, smooths out the rough edges, fills in gaps with something that feels true even if it isn’t entirely accurate. You remember how something felt more than how it actually was. Over time, those feelings become the story.

And stories, once formed, are hard to question.

There’s a certain comfort in believing your own narrative. It provides continuity, a sense of identity. You know who you are because you know the story you’ve been telling yourself. But that same comfort can become a kind of trap. If the story is too rigid, it leaves no room for revision. You become the person you’ve always said you were, even if that person no longer fits.

It’s strange how resistant we can be to changing our own stories.

Part of it, I think, is fear. Not the dramatic kind, but the quiet, persistent kind that shows up as hesitation. If you change the story, what replaces it? If you let go of a version of yourself, what fills the space it leaves behind? There’s no guarantee the new version will feel better, or even make sense.

So people hold on.

They hold on to outdated ideas about themselves, to habits that no longer serve them, to relationships that have quietly lost their balance. Not because they’re unaware, but because awareness doesn’t always translate into action. Sometimes it just sits there, uncomfortable and unresolved.

That’s another thing about that in-between hour—it doesn’t demand resolution. It allows things to remain open, at least for a little while. You can sit with a question without rushing toward an answer. You can acknowledge uncertainty without immediately trying to fix it.

There’s value in that.

We live in a culture that prioritizes answers. Quick ones, preferably. Efficiency is rewarded. Decisiveness is seen as strength. There’s very little space for lingering in uncertainty, for admitting that you don’t know what you think yet.

But uncertainty isn’t always a problem to solve. Sometimes it’s a space to explore.

Consider how many decisions are made simply to escape the discomfort of not knowing. Not because one option is clearly better than the other, but because choosing something—anything—feels better than remaining undecided. It gives the illusion of progress.

But movement isn’t always progress.

You can move in circles for a very long time without realizing it. The path feels active, even purposeful, but you’re returning to the same place again and again. It’s only when you pause—really pause—that you start to see the pattern.

Pausing, though, is difficult. It goes against the momentum of everything around you. There’s always something you could be doing, something you should be doing, something that feels just urgent enough to keep you from stopping.

And yet, stopping is often where clarity begins.

Not immediate clarity. Not the kind that arrives with a neat conclusion and a sense of closure. Real clarity is slower, quieter. It emerges gradually, often in fragments. A thought here, a realization there, pieces that don’t quite connect until they suddenly do.

It’s a subtle process. Easy to miss if you’re not paying attention.

That’s why noticing matters.

Noticing isn’t the same as observing. Observation is passive. It’s seeing what’s there without engaging with it. Noticing, on the other hand, involves a kind of presence. It requires you to register something, to let it land, even if you don’t fully understand it yet.

You notice when something feels off, even if you can’t explain why. You notice when a conversation leaves you unsettled, when a routine starts to feel hollow, when a place that once felt familiar begins to feel distant.

Those moments are easy to dismiss. It’s tempting to rationalize them away, to attribute them to temporary moods or external factors. Sometimes that’s accurate. But not always.

Sometimes those moments are signals.

Not clear instructions, not definitive answers—just signals. Indications that something is shifting, that something deserves your attention. They don’t tell you what to do, but they invite you to look more closely.

Looking more closely can be uncomfortable. It often reveals things you’d rather avoid. Patterns you didn’t intend to create. Choices you made without fully considering their consequences. Parts of yourself that don’t align with the story you’ve been telling.

But there’s also a kind of relief in it.

Because once you see something clearly, even if it’s difficult, you’re no longer operating in the dark. You’re no longer guessing. You have something to work with, something real.

And from there, change becomes possible.

Not inevitable, not immediate—but possible.

Change doesn’t require a dramatic overhaul. It rarely happens that way. More often, it starts small. A slight shift in perspective. A different response to a familiar situation. A willingness to question something you previously accepted without thinking.

These shifts don’t always feel significant at first. They might even feel inconsequential. But over time, they accumulate. They alter the trajectory in ways that are subtle but meaningful.

It’s like adjusting the direction of a path by a few degrees. At the beginning, the difference is barely noticeable. But as you continue, the distance between the old path and the new one grows.

Eventually, you end up somewhere entirely different.

There’s something both exciting and unsettling about that idea. It means your future isn’t fixed, that small choices matter more than they seem. But it also means there’s a level of responsibility that’s hard to ignore.

You can’t always control what happens, but you do have some influence over how you respond to it. Over time, those responses shape the direction of things.

That influence isn’t absolute. Circumstances, chance, and other people all play a role. But within that complexity, there’s still space for agency.

Recognizing that space is important.

It’s easy to fall into the belief that things just happen, that you’re being carried along by forces beyond your control. Sometimes that belief is comforting. It removes the pressure of decision-making, of responsibility.

But it can also be limiting.

Because if you believe you have no influence, you’re less likely to look for opportunities to change something, even when they exist. You become passive, not by necessity, but by assumption.

And assumptions are powerful.

They shape how you interpret the world, what you notice, what you ignore. They create boundaries that may not actually be there. Questioning them isn’t always easy, because they often feel like facts.

But they’re not.

They’re interpretations. And interpretations can be revised.

That revision process is rarely straightforward. It involves uncertainty, discomfort, and a willingness to let go of things that once felt stable. It’s not something people naturally gravitate toward.

But it’s often where growth happens.

Growth is another one of those words that sounds appealing in theory but feels complicated in practice. It implies improvement, progress, becoming something better. But it also involves change, and change can be disorienting.

There’s a tension there.

On one hand, there’s the desire to evolve, to move forward, to become more aligned with whatever you believe matters. On the other hand, there’s a pull toward familiarity, toward what’s known and predictable.

Balancing those forces isn’t easy.

Lean too far into change, and you risk losing a sense of stability. Lean too far into familiarity, and you risk stagnation. The balance point isn’t fixed; it shifts depending on circumstances, priorities, and timing.

Finding it requires attention.

Not constant analysis, not overthinking every decision, but a kind of ongoing awareness. A willingness to check in with yourself, to notice when something feels aligned or misaligned, to adjust accordingly.

It’s not a precise process. There’s no formula.

And maybe that’s the point.

Not everything needs to be optimized or perfected. Some things are meant to be experienced, explored, and understood gradually. They don’t resolve neatly, and they don’t have to.

There’s value in that kind of openness.

It allows for flexibility, for curiosity, for the possibility that you might be wrong about something—and that being wrong isn’t a failure, but an opportunity to see things differently.

That’s another thing people struggle with: the idea of being wrong.

It’s often framed as something to avoid, something that undermines credibility or confidence. But in reality, being wrong is an inevitable part of learning. It’s how perspectives expand, how understanding deepens.

The problem isn’t being wrong. It’s refusing to reconsider.

When you hold too tightly to a belief, even in the face of new information, you limit your ability to grow. You prioritize consistency over accuracy, certainty over truth.

Letting go of that rigidity doesn’t mean abandoning all structure or conviction. It means allowing for revision. It means recognizing that your current understanding is provisional, subject to change as you learn more.

That mindset creates space.

Space for new ideas, for different perspectives, for a more nuanced view of things. It makes conversations more meaningful, because they’re no longer about defending a position at all costs, but about exploring possibilities.

Exploration requires curiosity.

And curiosity, at its core, is about noticing—about paying attention to what’s interesting, what’s unclear, what doesn’t quite fit. It’s about asking questions, not necessarily to find immediate answers, but to deepen understanding.

That kind of curiosity tends to fade with time, especially when routines become established and responsibilities increase. There’s less room for wandering thought, for unstructured exploration.

But it doesn’t have to disappear entirely.

It can exist in small ways. In noticing something you’ve never paid attention to before. In asking a question you don’t already know the answer to. In allowing yourself to sit with an idea a little longer than usual.

Those moments might seem minor, but they add up.

They shape how you experience the world, how you interpret it, how you move through it. They influence the stories you tell yourself, and the ones you’re willing to question.

And sometimes, they lead you back to that in-between hour—the one that doesn’t belong to anyone, where things feel slightly undefined and full of possibility.

It’s not a place you can stay indefinitely. Eventually, night arrives. Decisions get made. Stories continue.

But for a little while, there’s space.

And in that space, there’s something quietly important: the chance to notice, to question, and maybe, just maybe, to choose a slightly different direction than the one you were on before.
:::writing

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
At the edge of a city that never quite decided what it wanted to be, there stood a building made of glass that always seemed a little dusty. It wasn’t tall enough to be impressive, not old enough to be historic, and not new enough to feel intentional. People passed it every day without remembering they had done so. If you asked someone to describe it, they might say, “Oh, the one with the reflections,” which described almost every building in the district.

Inside, on the fourth floor, there was an office where the lights flickered not because anything was broken, but because they were tired. Not metaphorically—if anything in this building could feel fatigue, it was the lights. They hummed faintly, as if reminding themselves they still had a job to do.

At a desk near the window sat a man named Ivo, who did not notice the lights, or the building, or even the city most days. He noticed patterns. Not grand, philosophical patterns—nothing that would earn him a book deal—but small ones. The way emails arrived in clusters at 9:07 and again at 11:42. The way his neighbor watered her plant every three days but always forgot on Sundays. The way the elevator hesitated for exactly half a second before opening on the fourth floor, as though considering whether it should.

Ivo worked in data reconciliation, which was a polite way of saying he found mistakes no one else wanted to find. He corrected numbers that almost matched, aligned columns that nearly agreed, and smoothed out discrepancies that would never be noticed by anyone who mattered. His work existed in the quiet space between “close enough” and “technically correct,” and he lived there comfortably.

On a Tuesday that felt like a Wednesday pretending to be a Monday, Ivo noticed something new.

At 10:13 a.m., an entry appeared in one of his datasets that did not belong. This was not unusual; things that did not belong were his entire profession. What was unusual was that this entry did not fail in any obvious way. It wasn’t malformed, didn’t break any rules, and didn’t trigger any alerts. It simply… didn’t make sense.

The value was a timestamp: 14:72:09.

Ivo stared at it for a long moment.

Minutes do not go to seventy-two.

He checked the raw data. Same value. He checked the system logs. No errors. He checked the upstream source. Clean. He even checked the documentation, which he rarely did, on the off chance that somewhere, somehow, time had been redefined without telling him.

Nothing.

He hovered his cursor over the entry, as if proximity might cause it to confess. It did not.

“Okay,” he said quietly, because saying things out loud sometimes made them easier to handle. “You’re wrong.”

The timestamp did not argue.

He corrected it to 14:12:09, assuming a transposition error, and moved on. There were always more discrepancies waiting politely in line.

At 11:42, the second cluster of emails arrived. One of them was from a system he didn’t usually hear from. The subject line read: RE: Adjustment Confirmed.

He hadn’t sent an adjustment request.

He opened the email.

It contained a single sentence: “Thank you for correcting the time.”

Ivo leaned back in his chair. The lights hummed. Somewhere down the hall, someone laughed too loudly at something that wasn’t that funny.

He checked the sender. It was a no-reply address tied to an internal system, one that handled archival processes. It did not send conversational emails. It barely sent emails at all.

“Glitch,” Ivo said, but he didn’t believe it.

He clicked through to the system interface. Nothing unusual. No record of the message being generated. No trace of the timestamp he had corrected. It was as if both had existed just long enough to be noticed and then quietly removed themselves from the record.

He considered telling someone. His manager, perhaps, who would nod thoughtfully and then suggest reopening the ticket with more detailed documentation. Or IT, who would ask him to reproduce the issue, which he could not.

Instead, he did what he always did: he paid attention.

At 1:03 p.m., while eating a sandwich that tasted like it had been assembled by someone who did not believe in sandwiches, he noticed the reflection in the window. Not his own—that was familiar and unremarkable—but something behind him.

The office was arranged in neat rows of desks, each one occupied by a person performing a variation of the same quiet labor. In the reflection, however, there was an empty desk where someone should have been sitting.

Ivo turned.

The desk was occupied. A woman sat there, typing steadily, her posture slightly hunched in the universal language of someone who had been at a computer too long.

He turned back to the window.

Empty.

He blinked, then turned again.

Occupied.

He watched the reflection carefully this time. The empty desk remained empty. No flicker, no hint of movement. Just absence, shaped like a person who should be there.

“Okay,” he said again, softer now.

He stood up and walked past the desk. The woman glanced up briefly, the way people do when someone enters their peripheral awareness but not their immediate concern.

“Hey,” Ivo said.

“Hey,” she replied.

“Have you been here all morning?”

She frowned slightly. “Yeah. Why?”

“No reason,” he said. “Just—checking something.”

She nodded, already returning to her screen.

Ivo went back to his desk and did not look at the window for the rest of his lunch.

At 2:17 p.m., another anomaly appeared.

This time, it was a name.

The dataset he was working on included a list of clients, each with a unique identifier and associated records. One entry had a name that did not match any known client.

It read: Ivo.

He felt a small, precise shift in his chest, like a gear catching.

He clicked into the record.

The details were sparse but specific. A series of timestamps—valid ones, this time—paired with brief notes.

08:02:11 – Arrived.

08:03:05 – Logged in.

10:13:00 – Noticed discrepancy.

10:14:22 – Corrected discrepancy.

11:42:07 – Received confirmation.

13:03:18 – Observed reflection mismatch.

Each entry was accurate to the second.

He scrolled.

There was one more.

14:17:00 – Not yet.

Ivo sat very still.

The office continued around him, unaware. Keys clicked. Chairs shifted. The lights hummed their tired hum.

He checked the system metadata. The record had been created at 14:16:59.

It was now 14:16:58.

He watched the seconds tick forward in the corner of his screen.

59.

14:17:00.

Nothing happened.

He let out a breath he hadn’t realized he was holding. “Okay,” he said, a little more forcefully this time. “Okay. That’s… fine.”

He waited.

At 14:17:09, the lights flickered.

Not the usual tired flicker, but something sharper, like a blink.

In that blink, the office changed.

It wasn’t dramatic. No one vanished, no walls shifted. It was the kind of change you would miss if you weren’t already looking for it.

The woman at the desk behind him was gone.

Not in a “she stood up and left” way. Her chair was pushed in, her computer asleep, her belongings absent. The desk looked as though it had not been used in weeks.

Ivo turned slowly, taking in the rows of desks. Something was off, but he couldn’t immediately name it. The spacing felt different. The pattern disrupted.

He counted.

There were fewer people.

Not by half, not by any obvious fraction. Just… fewer.

He stood up, his chair rolling back slightly. No one looked at him. No one seemed to notice anything was wrong.

He walked to the empty desk—the one that had been occupied moments ago, or perhaps always, depending on which version of events you trusted.

There was no sign anyone had ever been there.

“I talked to you,” he said, quietly, to the absence.

The absence did not respond.

He returned to his desk and looked at the screen.

The record with his name was gone.

In its place was a new discrepancy.

A timestamp: 14:17:09.

Valid.

Paired with a note: Adjusted.

Ivo sat down.

For the first time in a long while, he did not know what to correct.

The rest of the day passed in a kind of careful stillness. He continued his work, because stopping would have required explaining why, and he did not have an explanation that fit into the shapes available to him. But he watched.

He watched the reflections in the window, the patterns of movement, the small rhythms of the office. And once you start looking for changes, you begin to see how much of the world is held together by expectation rather than certainty.

At 3:28 p.m., a man in a blue shirt walked past his desk.

At 3:29 p.m., Ivo realized he had never seen that man before.

At 3:30 p.m., he realized that meant nothing.

At 4:05 p.m., an email arrived.

Subject: RE: Adjustment Confirmed.

Body: “Thank you for your attention to detail.”

Ivo did not open any systems. He did not check any logs. He simply stared at the message for a moment, then closed it.

At 5:00 p.m., he shut down his computer.

The elevator hesitated for its usual half second before opening on the fourth floor. Inside, a woman stood in the corner, looking at her phone. He glanced at her, then at the mirrored walls of the elevator.

In the reflection, he stood alone.

He smiled, just slightly.

“Okay,” he said, one last time.

The elevator doors closed.

When he stepped out onto the street, the city looked exactly as it always did—unfinished, undecided, full of buildings that reflected more than they revealed. People moved with purpose, or the appearance of it, each carrying their own small certainties.

Ivo paused on the sidewalk, watching the flow.

He had spent years correcting things that didn’t quite line up, believing that somewhere beneath the noise there was a clean, consistent truth waiting to be uncovered. But now, he wasn’t so sure.

Maybe the discrepancies weren’t errors.

Maybe they were edits.

He looked at his watch.

The time read 17:12:09.

He waited.

The seconds ticked forward, obedient and precise.

Nothing unusual happened.

Still, he found himself paying closer attention as he walked home, noticing the gaps between things—the moments where something could slip in or out without anyone realizing.

A missing person.

An extra second.

A reflection that didn’t agree.

Small adjustments.

He wondered, not for the first time that day, whether he had corrected the timestamp that morning… or whether he had created it.

The thought didn’t frighten him as much as it probably should have.

After all, it was just a discrepancy.

And discrepancies, he knew, were opportunities.

By the time he reached his apartment, the sky had settled into that uncertain shade between evening and night, where colors softened and edges blurred. He unlocked his door, stepped inside, and paused.

Something felt different.

Not wrong—just… adjusted.

He set his keys down on the table and looked around. Everything was where it should be. The chair by the window, the stack of books he kept meaning to read, the faint hum of the refrigerator.

He walked to the kitchen, opened a cabinet, and took out a glass. Filled it with water. Drank.

Normal.

He exhaled, a quiet release of tension he hadn’t fully acknowledged.

“Okay,” he said, softer now. Almost amused.

Then he noticed the clock on the wall.

It read 19:72:09.

Ivo stared at it, the corners of his mouth lifting just slightly.

“Not yet,” he said.

And this time, he didn’t reach to fix it.
:::writing

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
On the edge of the city, where the asphalt loosens its grip and the sidewalks forget to continue, there is a stretch of land that refuses to decide what it is. It is not quite wild and not quite tamed. A chain-link fence leans there like a tired sentry, its posts sunk at uncertain angles, its metal skin patched with mismatched repairs—silver here, rusted there, a square of chicken wire wired into place by hands that probably never returned. Beyond it, grass grows in contradictory moods: some patches lush and knee-high, others shaved to dust by invisible traffic. A narrow footpath cuts through the indecision, pressed into the ground by repetition, not permission.

If you follow that path, you pass a row of utility poles that hum softly when the air is damp. They carry lines that stretch toward somewhere important, though you cannot tell where from this vantage point. Birds perch on them without regard for voltage, tilting their heads as if listening for a story beneath the electrical whisper. Sometimes, in the late afternoon, the light hits the wires at such an angle that they vanish completely, leaving the birds suspended in midair, as if they have forgotten gravity or it has forgotten them.

At the end of the path stands a building that used to be something official. It has the shape of purpose even now—rectangular, symmetrical, made of poured concrete that has aged into a soft gray with darker streaks like dried tears. Windows line its front in a disciplined row, but most are broken, their jagged edges catching the light. Someone has boarded a few of them with plywood, though the boards have warped and peeled, revealing glimpses of the darkness inside. The door is gone. In its place is an opening that resembles a mouth mid-sentence.

People call it different things depending on how long they’ve lived nearby. The oldest residents say it was a substation. Others insist it was an administrative office for a project that never finished. A few claim it was a storage facility for records no one wanted to keep. The truth is less important than the fact that it exists, stubbornly occupying space that developers have circled on maps and then skipped, as if it emits a quiet refusal.

Inside, the air is cooler than outside, even in summer. The floor is a patchwork of cracked tiles, some missing entirely to reveal the darker substrate beneath. Dust lies everywhere, but not evenly—there are trails through it, arcs and lines that suggest movement, recent enough to notice, old enough to blur. The walls bear remnants of paint in a shade that might once have been optimistic, now dulled into something like resignation. Graffiti blooms in layers: names, symbols, declarations, each partially overwritten by the next person who needed to say something louder.

In one corner, there is a metal cabinet with its drawers half-open, as if caught in the act of offering or hiding. Inside are papers, yellowed and curling at the edges. The text on them is mostly illegible, the ink faded into ghostly impressions, but occasionally a word remains sharp enough to catch: “schedule,” “maintenance,” “temporary.” Words that imply continuation, responsibility, a plan. Words that no longer have a place to land.

There is also a staircase, narrow and steep, leading to a second level that is more suggestion than structure. The railing wobbles if you touch it. The steps creak in a language of complaint. At the top, the space opens into a room with fewer walls than it once had. Sections have been removed or collapsed, creating unexpected windows that frame the outside world in irregular shapes. Through one of these openings, you can see the city skyline in the distance, its buildings stacked like a promise that keeps revising itself.

People come here, though not in crowds. A teenager with a skateboard under his arm, pausing at the threshold as if deciding whether to enter a story or just a building. A woman with a camera, moving slowly, framing decay as if it were a kind of truth that needed documentation. A man who sits on the floor near the far wall, his back against the cool concrete, eyes closed, as though the silence here is a resource he cannot find elsewhere.

And sometimes there is a child, though never the same one twice. They appear in the afternoons, usually, when the light is slanting and the shadows stretch long enough to be interesting. They step inside without hesitation, as if the place has been waiting specifically for them. They explore with a kind of attention that is not cautious but not careless either—touching the walls, peering into corners, testing the echo of their own voice.

One such child, a girl with a bright red shoelace trailing from her right sneaker, came on a day when the sky was undecided between blue and gray. She paused just inside the doorway, letting her eyes adjust, then walked straight to the metal cabinet. She pulled open one of the drawers fully, ignoring the protest of rusted tracks, and sifted through the papers with quick, efficient motions.

“Nothing here,” she said to no one in particular, though her tone suggested she expected to be heard.

She moved on to the staircase, placing her feet carefully on each step, not because she feared falling but because she seemed to be listening to the sounds the steps made. At the top, she stood in the center of the room and turned slowly, taking in the broken walls, the distant skyline, the way the wind moved through the space as if it owned it.

“It’s not gone,” she said, more quietly this time.

From where she stood, there was a section of wall that had once been solid but now contained a rectangular opening at about waist height. It looked like a window that had been added as an afterthought, its edges rough, the concrete chipped unevenly. Beyond it was not the outside, as one might expect, but another room—smaller, darker, its doorway blocked by a fallen beam.

The girl approached the opening and crouched down, peering through. She reached inside, her arm disappearing into the shadow up to the elbow. For a moment, nothing happened. Then her expression changed, not into surprise exactly, but into recognition.

“Oh,” she said, as if greeting an old acquaintance. “You’re still here.”

If anyone else had been present, they might have seen only a child talking to an empty room. They might have assumed she was playing, inventing a companion to fill the silence. But there was a subtle shift in the air, a slight tightening, as if the building itself were paying attention.

“I thought you left,” she continued, her voice steady. “Everyone else did.”

There was a pause, during which the distant hum of the utility poles seemed to grow louder, or perhaps closer.

“No, I can’t,” she said after a moment, answering something unheard. “Not yet.”

She withdrew her arm and sat back on her heels, considering the opening with a seriousness that did not belong to her age. Then she stood and walked to the far side of the room, where a piece of chalk lay on the floor—white, worn down to a stub. She picked it up and returned to the opening, kneeling again.

“What do you remember?” she asked, and began to draw.

At first, the marks were simple lines, mapping the edges of the opening, tracing its irregular shape. Then she added more, extending the lines outward, creating a rough outline of the room beyond. She drew the fallen beam, the blocked doorway, the way the ceiling dipped slightly on one side. Her hand moved quickly, confidently, as if guided by something other than sight.

As she drew, the chalk made a faint scratching sound against the concrete, a sound that seemed to settle into the walls, to be absorbed and held. The air in the room shifted again, this time with a sense of anticipation, like a held breath.

“Like this?” she asked, pausing.

Another silence, but not empty. She nodded, as if receiving confirmation, and continued.

She added details that she could not have seen from her vantage point: a crack in the far wall shaped like a branching river, a small object in the corner that might have been a box or a stone, a pattern on the floor where tiles had been removed in a deliberate arrangement rather than by decay. Each addition seemed to clarify something not just on the wall but in the space itself, as though the act of drawing was also an act of remembering.

“Why didn’t you go?” she asked after a while, her voice softer.

This time, the pause was longer. The light shifted as a cloud passed overhead, dimming the room and then brightening it again. The girl sat back, resting her chalk hand on her knee, leaving a white imprint on the fabric of her pants.

“Oh,” she said finally, a hint of something like understanding in her tone. “You couldn’t.”

She looked at the drawing, at the lines that now covered a section of the wall around the opening, transforming it from a random break into something like a map.

“Do you want to?” she asked.

The question hung in the air, simple and enormous.

She waited, patient in a way that suggested she had done this before—not here, not with this building, but somewhere, somewhen, in a pattern that repeated across places and times. The building seemed to lean into the moment, its cracks and edges aligning in a subtle way, as if trying to listen more closely.

When the answer came—if it came—it did not arrive as a sound. It was a change in the quality of the silence, a shift from something dense and held to something lighter, more tentative.

“Okay,” the girl said, nodding once.

She stood and brushed the chalk dust from her hands, then looked around the room as if taking stock of what was available. Her gaze settled on a length of rope coiled in a corner, frayed at one end but otherwise intact. She walked over, picked it up, and tested its strength with a firm tug.

“Good enough,” she murmured.

She carried the rope back to the opening and threaded one end through, letting it drop into the dark room beyond. The rope uncoiled as it fell, making a soft whispering sound as it slid over the rough concrete edge.

“You’ll have to climb,” she said, a hint of apology in her voice. “I can’t come in there.”

She held the rope steady, bracing her feet against the floor, her small frame taking on a surprising solidity.

For a moment, nothing happened. Then the rope twitched, just slightly, as if something had touched it. The girl tightened her grip.

“That’s it,” she encouraged. “I’ve got you.”

The rope moved again, more noticeably this time, a slow, uneven tension traveling up its length. It was the movement of effort, of something unused to being used in this way. The girl leaned back, countering the pull, her arms steady.

“You’re doing it,” she said, her voice firm.

The air in the room shifted once more, the sense of anticipation giving way to something like release. The distant hum of the utility poles softened, or perhaps it was drowned out by the quiet, invisible struggle at the opening.

Inch by inch, whatever was on the other end of the rope seemed to make progress. The rope grew taut, then slack, then taut again, each cycle bringing it slightly higher.

“You can stop if you want,” the girl said at one point. “It’s your choice.”

The rope stilled for a moment, hanging in a delicate balance. Then it moved again, more decisively.

“Okay,” she said, almost to herself.

Finally, the end of the rope reached the edge of the opening. It lifted, slowly, as if guided by unseen hands, and then slipped over, falling onto the floor beside her with a soft thud.

The girl did not let go immediately. She held the rope for a few seconds longer, as if ensuring that whatever had climbed it had fully arrived. Then she relaxed her grip and let the rope drop.

She looked at the space in front of her, at the area just beyond the opening, and smiled—not broadly, not in a way that demanded attention, but with a quiet satisfaction.

“There you are,” she said.

If someone had been watching, they would have seen nothing new. The room remained empty, the air unchanged to the naked eye. But the girl’s posture shifted slightly, as if making room for another presence.

“Do you know where to go?” she asked.

Another pause, shorter this time.

“You can follow the path,” she suggested. “It leads out.”

She gestured vaguely toward the direction of the doorway, though her eyes remained focused on something at her side.

“Or you can stay for a bit,” she added. “It’s up to you.”

She listened, then nodded.

“Okay,” she said. “I’ll walk with you.”

She turned and headed toward the staircase, moving carefully but without the earlier deliberation. At the top of the stairs, she paused and glanced back at the wall with the drawing, at the opening that now seemed less like a mouth mid-sentence and more like a completed thought.

“Bye,” she said softly.

Then she descended, her footsteps echoing in the hollow building, accompanied by a second set of echoes that were not quite distinct but not entirely absent either.

Outside, the light had shifted toward evening, the undecided sky resolving into a clearer blue with streaks of gold near the horizon. The utility poles hummed their steady note, and the birds had returned to their wires, balancing effortlessly.

The girl stepped onto the path, the red shoelace still trailing behind her, and began to walk. She did not look back at the building, though she raised a hand briefly, as if acknowledging a farewell.

As she moved away, the building seemed to settle. The subtle tension that had held its cracks and edges in attentive alignment relaxed. The air within it grew still again, but not in the same way as before. There was a difference, slight but real, like a room after a long conversation has ended—quiet, but not empty.

The drawing on the wall remained, its chalk lines bright against the dull concrete. Over time, they would fade, smudged by wind and weather, by the occasional curious hand. The rope would be moved, perhaps taken by someone who found a use for it, or left to rot in place. The papers in the cabinet would continue their slow transformation into something closer to dust than document.

People would keep coming, in ones and twos, bringing their own stories, their own needs. Some would notice the drawing and wonder about it. A few might add their own marks, layering new lines over the old. Most would pass through without seeing anything beyond what was immediately visible.

And every so often, another child would arrive, drawn by something they could not name but recognized nonetheless. They would step inside, pause just long enough to feel the difference in the air, and then move with quiet purpose to wherever they were needed.

Because the building, for all its decay and ambiguity, had become a kind of threshold—not between places, exactly, but between states. It held, in its cracked walls and broken windows, the residue of things that had not fully resolved, the echoes of intentions that had nowhere else to go. And sometimes, when the conditions were right and the right person arrived, it offered a way through.

Not a grand passage, not a dramatic transformation. Just a rope lowered into a dark space, a steady hand on the other end, and the quiet decision to climb.

Out on the path, the girl walked until the building was no longer visible, until the utility poles gave way to streetlights and the uneven grass to more deliberate landscaping. The sounds of the city grew louder, more defined—cars passing, voices overlapping, the distant rhythm of something mechanical.

She reached down and tucked the loose end of her shoelace into her sneaker, securing it with a quick knot. Then she continued on, blending into the flow of people, her presence indistinguishable from any other child heading home at the end of the day.

If you had passed her on the street, you might have noticed nothing at all. Or perhaps you would have felt, for a brief moment, a sense of something just out of reach—a memory you couldn’t quite place, a thought that slipped away as soon as you tried to grasp it.

And if you had turned, just for a second, to look back, you might have seen her glance over her shoulder, not at you, but at something beside her, and nod, as if confirming that yes, this was the right way to go.

Then she would face forward again, and the moment would dissolve into the ordinary, leaving behind only the faintest impression that something had shifted, somewhere, in a way that mattered, even if you could not say exactly how.
:::writing

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
At the edge of a city that never quite decided what it wanted to be, there is a narrow strip of land where the pavement forgets itself and turns into dirt. It’s not a dramatic transition—no sudden cliff or wild burst of nature reclaiming concrete—but something softer, more hesitant. The sidewalk frays, the curb dips, and then, almost shyly, the earth begins. People pass it every day without noticing, or if they notice, they don’t assign it any importance. It’s just a leftover place. A margin. A space that belongs to no one in particular.

But if you stand there long enough, you begin to understand that it does belong—to time, mostly.

In the morning, the light reaches it first. The tall buildings nearby cast long shadows that stretch like tired limbs across the street, but this strip of land catches the early sun before the rest of the block fully wakes. The grass there is uneven, stubborn in patches, thin in others, interrupted by stones and small, unidentifiable bits of debris. A rusted bolt, half a tile, something that might once have been part of a toy. Dew gathers there overnight, and when the sun hits it, the whole place glimmers in a way that feels almost intentional, as if someone planned this quiet display and then forgot to tell anyone about it.

A man walks by each morning at nearly the same time. He doesn’t look at the strip directly, but he slows slightly when he reaches it. It’s subtle—so subtle that you might think it’s coincidence—but it happens every day. His shoes crunch differently on the ground there, even if he remains on the pavement. Perhaps it’s the shift in sound that makes him aware of it. Perhaps it’s something older, something memory-like, that nudges him to pay attention without understanding why.

He used to live somewhere very different, though he rarely speaks of it. There was more sky there. The kind of sky that makes you feel small in a way that isn’t unpleasant. The kind that doesn’t press down on you but instead opens outward, inviting you to imagine distance. There were fields—not perfectly maintained, not picturesque in the way postcards prefer, but real fields, with uneven growth and unexpected textures. If you walked through them, your shoes would pick up the scent of soil, and it would stay with you for hours, grounding you in a way that city air never quite manages.

He doesn’t consciously compare that place to this one. He doesn’t stand at the edge of the strip and think, This reminds me of something I’ve lost. But something in his body recognizes it. The slight irregularity. The way the ground refuses to be entirely shaped. The quiet insistence that not everything needs to be finished.

He passes by, as he always does, and the strip remains.

By midday, the city has asserted itself again. The sun is higher now, less gentle, and the shadows have retreated. Heat gathers in the pavement, radiating upward in a way that makes the air shimmer faintly. The strip of land looks less magical now, more ordinary, even slightly neglected. The grass loses its sparkle, the debris becomes more noticeable, and the unevenness feels less like character and more like disrepair.

A child stops there, though. Not because it is beautiful in any conventional sense, but because it is different. Children have a way of noticing these small fractures in the expected pattern of things. Where adults see inconvenience or neglect, children see possibility.

She crouches near the edge and begins to investigate. A stick becomes a tool, then a wand, then something else entirely. She traces lines in the dirt, creating shapes that have no fixed meaning. A road, perhaps. Or a boundary. Or just a motion repeated for the pleasure of repetition. She picks up a small stone and turns it over in her hand as if it might reveal something hidden on the other side.

Her guardian calls to her from a few steps away, reminding her that they have somewhere to be. There is always somewhere to be. The child lingers for a moment longer, reluctant to leave a space that seems to offer more than it promises. Then she stands, drops the stone, and runs to catch up.

The strip returns to stillness, but it has been altered in a small, almost invisible way.
