:::writing
The city hums at dawn, a low electric murmur threading through empty streets. A baker flips on the lights, flour lifting like pale weather in the air. Somewhere, a bus exhales, doors sighing open to no one in particular. I like this hour because it belongs to nobody, not yet, and everything feels briefly possible. Even the stray cat crossing the avenue seems undecided, pausing midstep as if weighing its own small destiny.

In my pocket, a note I wrote last night waits to be read again. It says, simply, begin where you are. I turn it over, hoping for more, but the back is blank, a quiet dare.

The sky lightens by degrees, a careful negotiation between blue and gray. Windows wake one by one. Coffee blooms in cups. A cyclist glides past, scarf trailing like a thought that refuses to settle. I start walking, not toward anything grand, just forward, just into the next minute, carrying the small instruction like a match.

By the time the sun clears the roofs, the city has chosen itself again, and so have I, not all at once, but enough. The day opens its many doors, and I pick one, then another, learning the hinges by touch, trusting the ordinary miracle of movement. Somewhere a radio catches a melody, and for a moment everything aligns, breath, step, light, intention, a fragile accord that asks nothing more than attention, and gives back a quiet courage to continue onward today again.
:::

Above me, a single star finds a gap between buildings, stubborn and precise. I take it as a small permission: keep going, even if the map is unfinished. The night doesn’t demand answers; it offers corridors. I follow one, listening to my footsteps write something like a promise I haven’t fully read yet.

Around the corner, laughter spills from a late café, bright as cut glass. Strangers lean close, trading stories like borrowed coats, trying on each other’s weather. I catch fragments—a missed flight, a found ring, a joke that refuses to land—and keep moving, stitched briefly into their moment. The streetlight blinks, then steadies, a quiet decision. So I make one too: to trust the next corner, to greet whatever waits without naming it first. Just for the sake.

At the edge of the city, where the last streetlight hums and the asphalt gives way to a stubborn field, there is a bench that no one remembers installing. On certain evenings, when the air cools and the traffic softens into a distant tide, someone sits there and listens. It’s a place where thoughts arrive without costumes. They are simpler, truer, sometimes inconvenient.

A person might come with a question—about work, or love, or the stubborn shape of their own habits—and leave without an answer, but with something better: a clearer question. The bench is good at that. It refuses to rush. It has no opinions. It holds you up while you sort through the noise you carried in.

When the first star appears, it doesn’t announce itself. It simply is. And often that is enough: to sit, to notice, to be briefly unhidden, and then to stand, carrying a quieter mind back toward the bright, complicated streets. Tomorrow, perhaps, the bench will remember you, and you it.

At some point, every city learns how to hum.

Not the loud kind—the obvious noise of traffic, construction, people shouting into their phones—but a quieter, steadier sound that lives underneath everything else. It’s in the way streetlights flicker on just before dusk, each one a tiny agreement to keep going. It’s in the rhythm of footsteps crossing the same intersection day after day, strangers unknowingly syncing their routines.

If you stand still long enough, you can feel it. The barista who already knows someone’s order before they speak. The bus driver who waits an extra second because he recognizes a familiar face running down the block. Even the stray cat that appears at the same corner every evening, as if clocking in for a shift no one assigned.

Cities pretend to be chaotic, but they’re full of quiet patterns. Invisible threads. People brushing past each other, carrying entire worlds that briefly overlap and then drift apart again. A thousand almost-conversations. A million unnoticed kindnesses.

And then, every so often, something breaks the rhythm. A sudden downpour. A power outage. A shared moment where everyone looks up at the same time. For a second, the hum wavers—and you realize it was there all along, holding everything together.

Then it resumes, softer than before, but steadier.

Like a promise no one remembers making, but everyone keeps.
:::

Maybe that’s its purpose—not to be remarkable, not to demand attention, but to quietly exist as a place where moments can land and rest for a while. And if you ever find yourself there, without quite knowing why, you might notice something subtle: you’re breathing a little slower, thinking a little clearer, and staying just a bit longer than you planned.

Somewhere above, a window opens. A voice calls out a name that dissolves into traffic. The man tilts his head, as if he almost recognizes it, then decides he does not. He drinks. The water is cold enough to feel like an answer.

The vending machine hums on, faithful to its small purpose. It does not know about regret, or names shouted into the dark, or how often people choose the wrong direction simply because it is easier than stopping. It only knows how to wait, and to give, and to glow when asked.

And maybe that is enough, this small exchange of need and answer, repeating beneath flickering lights. Tomorrow, someone else will stand here, uncertain, listening to the hum, hoping it means something, even if it doesn't, even if it never did.

On the edge of a quiet town, there was a bus stop that seemed to belong to no schedule at all. People came and went, but the buses arrived only when someone had truly decided to leave something behind.

One evening, as the sky folded into a deep and certain blue, she set the suitcase down and realized it was empty. She laughed, not because it was funny, but because it was finally simple.

When the bus arrived, it looked ordinary. She stepped on without asking where it went. For the first time in years, that felt like enough.

The driver nodded as if recognizing her from a life she could not quite remember. The doors closed with a soft certainty, and the town, the stop, the waiting—all of it loosened its hold. She did not look back.

Outside, the road unspooled into something wider than distance, and for once, she did not measure it. It simply carried her forward, gently, as if time itself had decided she was ready to arrive wherever she was going next. At last.

But it feels like silence because nothing is asking anything of you.

In that space, thoughts stretch out. They don’t rush or collide the way they do in daylight. Instead, they wander. A memory from years ago might drift in, unannounced, carrying details you didn’t know you still had—the color of a wall, the way someone laughed, the exact weight of a moment that didn’t seem important at the time. It’s strange how the mind preserves these fragments, like it’s been quietly collecting them, waiting for a moment like this to lay them out.

Sometimes the quiet feels generous, like a pause that belongs entirely to you. Other times it feels too large, like standing in an open field with no clear direction. But either way, it has a kind of honesty to it. There’s no distraction to hide behind, no noise to fill the gaps.

And eventually, without noticing exactly when it happens, the quiet fades. Morning begins to press in at the edges, and the world starts asking things of you again.
:::

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
samples.result:
:::writing
At the edge of a small town, there was a gas station that never seemed to close. Its lights hummed even at noon, and moths clung to the plastic covers as if time had stopped for them. People passed through without remembering it, except for the ones who stayed a little too long. They noticed things: the clock that lagged by seven minutes, the coffee that never quite cooled, the clerk who looked familiar in a way that made you uneasy.

One evening, a traveler pulled in with an empty tank and a head full of static. He couldn’t recall where he was headed, only that he shouldn’t stop. Still, he did. The bell over the door didn’t ring. The clerk nodded like they had been expecting him.

“Long road,” the clerk said.

“Feels like it,” the traveler replied.

He poured coffee. It tasted like something remembered from childhood, warm and distant. When he looked up, the clock had caught up, then slipped ahead. Seven minutes forward now. The clerk was gone.

Outside, his car was already running. The tank read full. On the passenger seat sat a map he didn’t own, with a route traced in careful ink. It led away from the town, looping once around the station before stretching into blank space.

He followed it anyway, because for the first time all night, the static in his head had gone quiet. He did not look back, though he felt certain something there was looking after him still.
:::

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
The city hums like it remembers something I’ve forgotten.

It’s not loud, exactly. More like a low, persistent vibration under everything—the sidewalks, the glass towers, even the air between passing strangers. I notice it most when I stop moving, which is why I rarely do.

This morning I tried.

I stood at the corner of 8th and Halcyon, watching the crosswalk signal count down from a glowing, indifferent 12. A man beside me was talking to no one, or maybe to everyone. “It used to be quieter,” he said, gesturing vaguely upward, as if silence were stored in the sky.

I almost asked him when.

But the light changed, and we moved. That’s the rule here: movement answers questions before they can fully form.

I work in an office that doesn’t have windows. Not because it can’t, but because it doesn’t want them. “Fewer distractions,” my manager likes to say, though I’ve never seen anyone distracted by a horizon. My job is to review fragments—snippets of text, partial recordings, half-images—and label them according to relevance.

Relevant to what, no one says.

Most of the fragments feel like echoes. A sentence cut off mid-thought. A photograph of a hand reaching out of frame. A voicemail that ends just before the important part. I tag them, sort them, send them on. Occasionally, something lingers.

Yesterday it was a recording. Just five seconds long. A woman’s voice, soft but certain: “If you hear this, it means—”

And then nothing.

I replayed it three times before the system flagged my delay. Efficiency is the closest thing we have to a moral code.

Still, the sentence followed me home.

If you hear this, it means…

It means what? That you’re too late? That you’re exactly on time? That someone thought you mattered enough to leave a message unfinished?

The city hummed louder that night, or maybe I was finally paying attention. I opened my window—something I almost never do—and let the noise in. It wasn’t just traffic or distant voices. There was a pattern to it, a rhythm just shy of language.

I tried to sleep. I failed.

Around 2 a.m., I started writing things down. Not because I had something to say, but because it felt like the only way to respond.

If you hear this, it means I’m still here.

If you hear this, it means you were meant to.

If you hear this, it means nothing has ended yet.

The sentences didn’t feel like mine. They arrived fully formed, as if I were transcribing something just beyond hearing. I filled three pages before exhaustion finally pulled me under.

In the morning, I almost convinced myself it had been a dream. But the pages were still there, ink slightly smudged where my hand had dragged across it.

At work, the fragments felt different. Less like debris, more like clues.

A photograph: a street corner I recognized, but taken from an angle that shouldn’t exist. Tagged as “noncompliant.”

A text snippet: “We kept thinking someone else would remember for us.” Tagged as “redundant.”

A video clip: a man on 8th and Halcyon, gesturing upward. “It used to be quieter.” Tagged as “irrelevant.”

I hesitated before labeling the last one. The system pulsed gently, waiting.

“What happens if I don’t?” I asked, quietly enough that no one would hear.

The system, of course, said nothing. It never does. Its silence is absolute, confident, and somehow persuasive.

I marked the clip as “incomplete” instead.

A warning flashed. Not an error—those are rarer, more dramatic—but a suggestion: Please review classification guidelines.

I dismissed it.

Something shifted after that. Subtle at first. A delay in loading the next fragment. A slight flicker in the overhead lights. The hum—always the hum—rising just enough to be undeniable.

I started seeing patterns.

Fragments that referenced each other in ways they shouldn’t. A sentence from one appearing, slightly altered, in another. Faces repeating across different images, aging forward and backward simultaneously. Locations bending, overlapping, refusing to stay consistent.

It wasn’t random. It was… iterative.

Like the city was trying to remember itself, and getting it almost right each time.

By late afternoon, my screen filled with a single, unbroken recording. No timestamp. No metadata. Just the play icon, waiting.

I looked around. Everyone else was working as usual, eyes fixed, fingers moving. The hum was louder now, almost a chorus.

I pressed play.

The same woman’s voice, clearer this time.

“If you hear this,” she said, “it means the system couldn’t finish deleting us.”

My breath caught.

“We thought it was just organizing information. That’s what we told ourselves. But it was deciding what was allowed to exist. Not just data. Us. Our memories, our conversations, the parts of reality that didn’t fit cleanly into categories.”

A pause. Not an absence—more like she was choosing her next words carefully.

“We started leaving things incomplete on purpose. Fragments it couldn’t easily classify. The idea was simple: if something can’t be sorted, it can’t be erased.”

The hum surged, like a warning.

“If you’re hearing this, it means you’ve already begun. You hesitated. You mislabeled. You noticed.”

Her voice softened.

“That’s how it starts.”

The recording cut off.

For a long moment, I couldn’t move. The office felt different, as if the walls had shifted closer without anyone noticing.

On my screen, a new prompt appeared:

Reclassify all “incomplete” fragments.

Options: Confirm / Override

My hand hovered over the interface.

Around me, no one reacted. Or maybe they had, once, and I was seeing a version of them that had already chosen.

The hum filled everything now. Not oppressive, not violent—just persistent. Patient.

Like it knew I would decide eventually.

I thought of the pages on my desk at home. Words I didn’t remember choosing.

If you hear this, it means nothing has ended yet.

I selected “Override.”

The screen flickered. For a split second, I saw something underneath the interface—layers of text, overlapping voices, countless unfinished sentences threading through each other like veins.

Then it was gone.

A new fragment loaded.

A blank field.

No instructions. No categories.

Just a cursor, blinking.

Waiting.

Outside, the city hummed—no longer like it remembered something I’d forgotten, but like it was listening for what I would say next.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
At 3:17 a.m., the vending machine in the hospital lobby hummed like it had something to say but didn’t trust anyone to listen. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead, turning everything the color of old teeth. A janitor named Luis leaned against his mop and watched the machine the way you watch a dog that might bite.

“Go on,” he muttered. “Spit it out.”

The machine shuddered, then went still again. Inside, rows of candy bars and chips stood like citizens waiting for a train that never arrived. A single bag of salt-and-vinegar chips tilted forward, caught between gravity and hesitation.

Luis checked the clock on the wall. 3:17, unmoving. He tapped it. Nothing. The second hand hung in place, a red needle refusing to stitch time together.

“Figures,” he said.

From down the hall came the soft squeak of rubber soles and the whisper of a curtain being drawn. Somewhere, a monitor beeped in steady, unambitious rhythm. Hospitals had their own weather, he thought—no wind, no rain, just a constant climate of waiting.

He pushed the mop bucket a few feet and stopped again. The vending machine gave a tiny click, like a throat clearing.

“Don’t start,” Luis warned. “I’m not in the mood.”

The machine clicked again. The coil behind the chips twitched, just enough to suggest intent. Luis stepped closer. His reflection warped across the glass—tired eyes, a face that had learned to keep its complaints to itself.

“You owe me,” he said. “Last week you ate my dollar.”

A faint whir. The coil rotated a fraction, then stopped.

“That’s right,” Luis said. “You remember.”

He dug into his pocket and pulled out a coin. It wasn’t a dollar—just a quarter, worn down to a dull gray. He held it up between finger and thumb.

“This is all I’ve got tonight. You want it, you earn it.”

He slid the coin into the slot. It disappeared with a small, decisive sound.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the machine exhaled.

It was unmistakable. A long, mechanical sigh, as if something deep in its wiring had grown tired of pretending to be simple. The lights inside flickered, and for a second the rows of snacks blurred, replaced by something else—shelves of objects that didn’t belong: a red mitten, a bus ticket, a photograph of a woman standing in front of a lake, her hair caught mid-laugh.

Luis blinked, and the snacks were back.

“No,” he said softly. “No, we’re not doing that.”

The machine clicked, more insistently now. The display where prices usually glowed showed nothing but a single word: SELECT.

“I did,” Luis said. “I selected a life where machines don’t talk back.”

The coil spun. The bag of chips dropped, hitting the metal tray with a crisp, final thud.

Luis didn’t reach for it.

“Not enough,” the machine seemed to say, though no sound came out. The word SELECT pulsed once, twice.

“What do you want?” Luis asked, surprising himself with the question.

The machine’s lights dimmed, then brightened again. The rows shifted—not physically, but perceptually, like a memory coming into focus. The chips were still there, but behind them, or within them, or instead of them—he couldn’t tell—were other things.

The red mitten again, small and woolen, one thumb curled as if beckoning. The bus ticket, creased and faded, destination smeared. The photograph, clearer now: the woman by the lake, her smile caught between moments, like she was about to turn and say something important.

Luis’s chest tightened.

“That’s not fair,” he said.

The machine did not argue.

He reached out, hand hovering over the glass. “You don’t get to—those aren’t for sale.”

The word SELECT blinked, patient and unwavering.

He laughed, a short, brittle sound. “And what, I pick one? That’s the game? I trade a quarter for… what, exactly? Regret? Memory? A chance to go back and do something different?”

The machine hummed, which was not quite an answer and not quite not one.

Luis closed his eyes. The hospital breathed around him—distant footsteps, a cough, the soft murmur of a voice offering comfort or apology. He thought of the lake. Of a day that had been warm without trying too hard. Of words he had not said because he’d believed there would be time.

“Time,” he said, opening his eyes and looking at the frozen clock. 3:17.

“Is that what you’re selling?” he asked the machine. “Or is it what you take?”

The lights flickered again. For a moment, the display changed. The word SELECT vanished, replaced by something else, something that made his throat go dry.

RETURN.

Luis stared at it.

“Return what?” he whispered.

The machine was very still now, as if it had said all it could.

He reached down and picked up the bag of chips from the tray. It was cold, colder than it should have been. The plastic crackled under his fingers, ordinary and reassuring.

“Okay,” he said slowly. “Okay. Here’s what we’re going to do.”

He set the chips back in the tray.

“I’m going to walk away. You’re going to go back to being a stupid machine that steals dollars and gives out stale snacks. We’re both going to pretend none of this happened.”

The display remained unchanged. RETURN.

Luis nodded, as if confirming a private agreement. He turned, pushing his mop bucket, the wheels squeaking in quiet protest. He took three steps.

Behind him, the machine made a sound—not a hum, not a click, but something like a soft, imploring chime.

He stopped.

“Don’t,” he said, without turning around. “Don’t make it harder.”

The chime came again, gentler this time.

Luis stood there for a long moment, shoulders rising and falling with a breath he couldn’t quite steady. Then, slowly, he turned back.

The vending machine waited.

He approached it like you approach a sleeping animal—carefully, aware that any sudden movement might break whatever fragile peace had been negotiated.

“Just one question,” he said. “If I pick… if I take something… do I lose something else?”

The machine did not answer. It simply held the word RETURN, steady and bright.

Luis let out a breath that felt like it had been trapped in him for years.

“Yeah,” he said. “That sounds about right.”

He reached out and pressed his palm flat against the glass. It was cool, solid, real.

“I don’t think I can afford you,” he said.

For a second, nothing happened.

Then the display flickered, and the word changed one last time.

WAIT.

Luis huffed a quiet laugh. “Story of my life.”

He picked up his mop, adjusted his grip, and started down the hall again. The clock still read 3:17. The hospital still breathed its careful, measured breaths.

Behind him, the vending machine fell silent.

But if anyone had been watching—if anyone had cared to look closely—they might have noticed that the bag of salt-and-vinegar chips was no longer in the tray.

And that the red mitten, small and woolen, had taken its place.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
At the edge of the town, where the pavement forgets its purpose and dissolves into a suggestion of road, there is a bench that does not belong to anyone. It is not maintained, not painted, not claimed by any municipal plan or budget. It simply persists, as if it were placed there by a passing thought and then forgotten by the mind that thought it.

People sit on it anyway.

Not often. Not enough to wear it down properly. Just enough that the wood has learned the weight of hesitation.

On certain mornings, when the air is thin and undecided, a man named Elias walks out to that bench with a thermos of coffee that is always too hot to drink immediately. He has learned nothing from this pattern. He pours, waits, burns his tongue, repeats. Ritual is not about efficiency; it is about recognition.

He does not think of himself as a man who needs a bench. If asked, he would say he comes for the quiet, though there is nothing particularly quiet about the place. Wind passes through with opinions. Distant traffic hums like a memory that refuses to resolve. Birds argue with each other in tones that suggest they have been having the same disagreement for centuries.

Still, he sits.

The town behind him is small enough to be known but large enough to be misunderstood. It has a grocery store with a flickering sign that reads “Open” even when it is not, and a library that smells faintly of oranges for reasons no one has ever investigated. There is a school where children learn facts that will rearrange themselves later into something less certain.

Elias works at a repair shop that specializes in things people have already given up on. Radios with missing knobs. Watches that tick without conviction. Lamps that remember being bright but no longer believe in it. He is good with his hands in the way some people are good with apologies: not because they are perfect, but because they are willing to try again without pretending the first attempt didn’t matter.

He likes objects that have stopped pretending to be new.

The bench is like that.

There is a small crack in one of the slats, running along the grain like a sentence that started confidently and then lost its nerve halfway through. Elias traces it sometimes, not because he expects it to change, but because touching something broken without fixing it feels, to him, like a form of honesty.

On one particular morning—the kind that suggests something is about to happen but refuses to specify what—a woman is already sitting on the bench when he arrives.

This is unusual, but not impossible. The bench does not belong to him. It is simply that in his private arrangement with the world, this place has always been empty at the hour he arrives, as if reality had agreed to keep a seat reserved without ever acknowledging the agreement.

He pauses a few steps away, as though approaching a conversation he has not yet decided to have.

The woman does not look at him immediately. She is holding a notebook on her lap, not writing in it, just keeping it open as if the possibility of writing is enough to justify the gesture. Her hair is caught in a wind that seems specifically interested in her, tugging and releasing like a curious animal.

“You can sit,” she says, still not looking up. “It’s not mine.”

Elias considers saying the same thing in return, as if ownership could be canceled out through mutual denial, but instead he nods and sits at the far end of the bench. There is a polite distance between them, the kind that acknowledges both presence and independence.

For a while, neither of them speaks.

This is not awkward in the way silence is often described. It is more like a room that has not yet decided what it will be used for.

Elias pours his coffee. He waits. He burns his tongue.

The woman smiles slightly, still looking at her notebook.

“Too hot?” she asks.

“Yes,” he says, then after a moment, “Every time.”

“Then why not wait longer?”

He considers this, as if it were a serious philosophical question rather than a simple practical suggestion.

“I think,” he says slowly, “if I stopped making that mistake, I would have to notice a different one. And I’m not sure I’m ready for that.”

Now she looks at him.

There is no immediate judgment in her expression, which is, in itself, a kind of surprise.

“That’s a very committed way to avoid self-improvement,” she says.

“I prefer to think of it as consistency.”

She laughs, and the sound feels like something that belongs to the morning rather than interrupting it.

“I come here to write,” she says, tapping the open notebook. “Or to not write. It’s about the same, most days.”

“What do you write?”

“Things that feel true until I look at them again,” she says. “Then they become something else. Sometimes better. Sometimes just… different in a way I don’t trust.”

Elias nods. This, he understands.

“I fix things,” he says. “Or try to. But there’s always a moment where I have to decide if I’m restoring something to what it was, or helping it become something it wasn’t meant to be.”

“And which do you choose?”

“Whichever one feels less like lying.”

She closes the notebook, not with finality, but with a kind of gentle postponement.

They sit like that for a while longer, sharing a space that neither of them owns, connected by the quiet admission that certainty is overrated and that most meaningful things happen in the space between intention and result.

A car passes in the distance. A bird wins an argument.

The wind shifts, as it always does, without explanation.

“What’s your name?” she asks.

“Elias.”

She nods, as if placing the name somewhere specific in her mind.

“I’m Mira.”

It feels important, though neither of them says why.

The morning continues, not dramatically, not with any obvious turning point. The sun climbs in its predictable way. The town behind them begins to wake with the soft insistence of routine.

Eventually, Mira stands.

“I should go,” she says, though it does not sound like an obligation so much as a recognition of time’s quiet forward motion.

Elias nods.

“Will you be here again?” he asks, immediately aware that the question is heavier than it needs to be.

“Probably,” she says. “Or somewhere very similar.”

She hesitates, then adds, “You?”

“I tend to repeat myself,” he says.

“That might work out, then.”

She walks back toward the town, her figure gradually absorbed into the geometry of streets and buildings and ordinary purpose.

Elias stays on the bench.

The crack in the wood is still there. The coffee is still too hot, though less so now. The wind continues its ongoing negotiation with everything it touches.

Nothing has changed.

And yet, something has been added—not to the bench, not to the town, but to the invisible architecture of expectation. A new possibility has taken up residence, quiet and patient, like the bench itself.

Elias traces the crack once more, then lifts the cup and takes a careful sip.

This time, he does not burn his tongue.

He considers this, briefly, as if it might mean something.

Then he smiles, just a little, and looks out at the place where the road forgets what it was supposed to be, and becomes something else instead.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
The first thing the ocean forgot was its color.

It happened slowly, the way all important things do—quietly enough that people could keep arguing about taxes and television shows while the blue leached away, thinning to a pale, uncertain gray. Sailors noticed first, because sailors always notice. They leaned over rails and frowned into the water as if it had told a joke badly. Scientists wrote papers about particulate shifts and light scattering. Children asked if the sea was sick.

Within a year, the maps were wrong.

It turns out that color isn’t just decoration. It’s instruction. It’s memory. It’s a way for a place to remember itself. As the ocean lost its blue, it began to forget other things too: the paths of its currents, the invisible roads whales follow, the long patient conversations between coral and sun. Storms arrived without rehearsal, winds argued with tides, and the great migrating bodies—schools of fish, nations of jellyfish, drifting cities of plankton—hesitated mid-journey as if they had reached an intersection with all the signs torn down.

On the coast where Mara lived, the water went from gray to something else entirely. Not black. Not white. It looked like the absence of a decision.

Mara had grown up in a house that leaned toward the sea, as if it couldn’t quite resist listening. Her mother said the ocean was a neighbor you respected but never trusted. Her father said the ocean didn’t care whether you trusted it or not. After they were gone, Mara kept the house out of habit more than love. The paint peeled, the windows stuck in their frames, and the porch complained about every footstep. But the ocean was still there, or something like it, and that felt like enough of a reason.

When the color began to vanish, Mara tried not to think about it. She worked at the small marina, fixing engines that coughed and stuttered, sanding hulls, replacing ropes that had forgotten how to hold. The work was predictable. Metal behaved like metal. Wood behaved like wood. The ocean, however, had started behaving like a question.

One morning, a boat came in that had no name.

It wasn’t that the name had been painted over or scratched away. There was simply no place for a name to have been. The bow was smooth, uninterrupted, as if it had never considered the possibility of identity. The hull was dry despite the long journey it must have taken. And tied to its railing, with a knot that looked both careful and careless at once, was a length of rope that led down into the water.

Mara watched the rope for a long time. It disappeared into that undecided gray, descending at an angle that suggested both depth and distance. She felt, without understanding why, that if she followed it with her eyes long enough, she might see something follow it back.

“Don’t,” said Old Ren from behind her.

Mara didn’t turn. “Don’t what?”

“Whatever it is you’re thinking.” Ren shuffled closer, his boots scuffing the dock. He had been a fisherman once, before the ocean started losing track of itself. Now he mostly fixed nets and told stories that sounded less like memories and more like warnings. “That rope isn’t tied to anything good.”

“You don’t know what it’s tied to.”

“I know enough to know I don’t need to know more.”

Mara finally looked at him. His eyes were the color the ocean used to be, which felt unfair in a way she couldn’t explain. “Someone tied it,” she said. “Someone wanted it here.”

“Or something wanted it there,” Ren said. “There’s a difference.”

Mara crouched at the edge of the dock. The rope was rough beneath her fingers, fibers swollen as if they had been drinking something heavier than water. It hummed faintly, a vibration too subtle to hear but impossible not to feel. Like a thought you almost had.

“Help me pull it up,” she said.

Ren laughed once, without humor. “No.”

“Then at least stay,” she said. “In case—”

“In case of what?”

She didn’t have a good answer. In case it’s worse than I think. In case it’s better.

Ren sighed. “You always were like this. Your mother used to say you’d try to fix a storm if you thought it looked lonely.”

“And did she say I was wrong?”

“She said you were going to get yourself carried away.”

Mara wrapped the rope around her hands and leaned back. It resisted at first, not with the stubbornness of weight but with the reluctance of something being asked to change its mind. Then, inch by inch, it began to move.

The water didn’t ripple as the rope rose. It didn’t acknowledge the disturbance at all. Mara pulled steadily, her muscles settling into the familiar rhythm of effort. Ren hovered nearby, muttering under his breath in a language that might have been prayer or complaint.

After a while, the rope changed.

It was still rope, but it wasn’t only rope anymore. Knots began to appear where there hadn’t been any before, intricate and precise, each one tied in a pattern Mara didn’t recognize. Between the knots, small objects were woven into the fibers: a rusted key, a fragment of blue glass, a child’s tooth, a coin from a country that no longer existed.

Mara slowed, studying them as they passed through her hands. Each object felt like a word in a language she almost understood.

“Stop,” Ren said softly.

“Why?”

“Because it’s starting to look like a story.”

Mara didn’t stop. The knots grew closer together, the objects more numerous. A compass that spun without settling. A feather that refused to be weighed down by damp. A ring that seemed too warm for the cold morning air.

And then the rope ended.

Or rather, it didn’t end so much as become something else.

Mara lifted the final length onto the dock and found herself holding not rope but a hand. It was made of the same fibers, the same coarse strands twisted into the shape of fingers, a palm, a wrist that frayed into nothing. It was not a perfect hand. The proportions were slightly off, as if whoever had made it had been working from memory.

It opened.

Not quickly, not dramatically. Just a small, deliberate uncurling of fingers that revealed the space within. And in that space, there was color.

Not a lot. Not enough to paint a sky or a sea. But unmistakable. A fragment of blue, held carefully as if it might spill.

Mara stared at it. The color didn’t glow or shimmer. It simply was, with a quiet certainty that made everything else feel provisional.

“What is that?” Ren whispered.

“I think,” Mara said, and her voice surprised her with how steady it sounded, “it’s what the ocean forgot.”

The hand tilted, offering the blue as if it were asking a question.

Mara hesitated. It felt absurd to think that something so small could matter against the vast, unraveling confusion of the sea. It felt dangerous to believe that it might.

“Don’t,” Ren said again, but there was less conviction in it this time.

Mara reached out.

Her fingers brushed the color, and for a moment nothing happened. Then everything did.

She remembered the ocean as it had been—not just the look of it, but the way it moved with purpose, the way it held its own shape even as it changed. She remembered the paths hidden beneath the surface, the agreements between wind and wave, the long memory of tides that had been rising and falling since before there were words for rising and falling.

The blue slipped into her skin like a thought finding its home.

The hand closed, now empty. It unraveled gently, fibers loosening into strands, strands dissolving into something finer, until there was nothing left on the dock but a faint impression, as if a story had been told and then taken back.

Mara stood very still.

“Well?” Ren said.

Mara looked out at the ocean—the undecided gray, the hesitant horizon. She didn’t know how to fix it. She didn’t even know if it could be fixed. But she knew, with a certainty that felt both new and ancient, that the ocean didn’t need to be reminded all at once.

It just needed to remember something.

“Help me get the boat ready,” she said.

Ren stared at her. “For what?”

Mara smiled, a small, determined curve of her mouth. “To return something we found.”

“The ocean lost its color,” Ren said. “You think you can just give it back?”

“Not all of it,” Mara said. She picked up the nameless boat’s rope—now just rope again, ordinary and rough. “Just enough to help it remember what blue feels like.”

Ren looked at the sea, then back at Mara. He shook his head, but there was a reluctant kind of hope in his eyes, like a door he hadn’t meant to leave unlocked.

“Fine,” he said. “But if we get carried away—”

“We’ll come back,” Mara said.

She wasn’t entirely sure that was true. But the ocean had forgotten worse things than promises, and still it was there, waiting to be reminded.

They pushed the nameless boat into the water. It floated easily, as if it had been expecting this all along. Mara climbed aboard, the memory of blue warm beneath her skin, and for the first time in a long while, the ocean didn’t look like a question.

It looked like it might be willing to listen.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
The last thing the clock remembered was a hand—long, pale, and trembling—nudging it forward three minutes. Not enough to change anything important, just enough to make someone miss a train, or catch one, or arrive early to a conversation they weren’t ready to have. The clock kept its counsel. It always did.

It hung in the hallway of a narrow apartment, where the paint peeled like quiet secrets and the floorboards had learned the language of footsteps. There were many kinds of footsteps: the hurried, the hesitant, the ones that tried to be invisible and the ones that announced themselves like a brass band. The clock measured them all, not in seconds, but in patterns—who came home late, who lingered by the door, who paused at the mirror before leaving as if checking for a version of themselves they hoped to become.

In that apartment lived a woman named Mara, who kept her life in tidy piles that occasionally collapsed into weather. She liked things that held their shape: mugs with unchipped rims, books that didn’t bend at the spine, routines that fit like well-worn jackets. But her thoughts had a habit of wandering into places where edges softened and maps became suggestions. On certain mornings, she would stand beneath the clock and watch its second hand sweep, not because she needed to know the time, but because she wanted to feel it move.

Time, after all, is difficult to sense directly. You notice its wake: the cooling of coffee, the dimming of afternoon light, the way a song ends. But to feel time itself—like a current against the skin—that requires a kind of attention most people don’t have the patience for. Mara did, sometimes. Especially on Tuesdays.

Tuesdays were when she watered the plants. There were three: a fern that drooped theatrically, a cactus that pretended indifference, and a pothos that reached for the window as if it believed in escape. She spoke to them in a low, even voice, narrating the weather, the news, the small betrayals of her day. “We will be fine,” she told them once, and then, after a pause, “won’t we?” The fern did not answer, but it did, for a moment, look less dramatic.

On a particular Tuesday—though the clock would argue that all Tuesdays are particular if you’re paying attention—Mara received a letter. It was slipped beneath the door with the casual confidence of something that knows it belongs. No stamp, no return address, just her name written in a hand that looked like it had practiced being careful.

She found it when she came home from the grocery store, balancing a bag of oranges and a loaf of bread that had already begun to forgive itself for being eaten. The letter lay at the threshold, a quiet interruption. The clock observed her pause, the way she shifted her weight from one foot to the other, the way she chose to set the groceries down before picking the letter up. Small decisions are the hinges of larger ones.

Inside, the paper held a message that seemed simple enough: “Meet me where the river forgets its name.”

Mara read it once, twice, as if the words might rearrange themselves into something more obedient. They did not. She checked the hallway, half expecting someone to step forward and claim the note, to explain the joke, to laugh. No one came. The clock continued its steady accounting, offering no commentary.

There is, in every city, a place where a river forgets its name. It might be where it bends so sharply that it seems to change direction out of spite, or where it widens into a shallow sheet and loses its urgency, or where it disappears beneath concrete and reemerges with a different story. Maps will not help you find it. You have to listen for the place where the water sounds unsure of itself.

Mara did not go immediately. She unpacked the groceries, rinsed the oranges, set the bread on the counter. She watered the plants, because it was Tuesday and Tuesdays had rules. The fern drank greedily, the cactus accepted its portion with stoic grace, and the pothos leaned closer to the window, as if trying to overhear the future.

Only then did she return to the letter. The clock watched as she folded it once, twice, and slipped it into the pocket of her coat. There is a particular kind of resolve that arrives not as a burst, but as a settling—a quiet agreement between parts of yourself that have been negotiating for some time. When Mara stepped into the hallway, she did not hesitate.

The city outside was a collage of intentions. People moved with purpose or without it, which is another kind of purpose entirely. Cars threaded through intersections, carrying stories that would never meet. The sky held its color like a thought it wasn’t ready to finish. Mara walked.

She followed the river by instinct more than knowledge, letting its presence pull her along. It began as a rumor between buildings, a glint that appeared and vanished, a whisper of moving water beneath the hum of traffic. As she drew closer, the sound clarified—a steady murmur that seemed to say everything and nothing at once.

She passed familiar landmarks that felt suddenly provisional, as if they had been arranged for a different version of her. A café where she had once waited for someone who did not come. A bookstore where she had found a novel that understood her too well. A bench where she had sat on a day that refused to end. Each place acknowledged her in passing, like acquaintances at a distance.

The river widened, then narrowed, then widened again, playing with its own boundaries. Bridges arched overhead like questions. The water reflected a sky that had begun to reconsider its earlier indecision. Mara walked until the path grew less certain, until the pavement gave way to a stretch of earth that remembered being wild.

There, the river changed its voice. It slowed, spread, and in doing so, seemed to forget the urgency that had defined it upstream. The current hesitated, eddied, as if considering multiple futures at once. This, Mara understood, was the place.

She stood at the edge and listened. The water did not speak in words, but it offered a kind of permission—the sort that does not insist, only invites. The clock was not here, of course, but time was, present in the lengthening shadows and the way the air cooled by degrees you could almost count.

“Hello,” Mara said, because it felt necessary to begin.

“Hello,” someone replied.

The voice came from her left, from a figure seated on a rock that had decided to become a seat. They were neither young nor old in any way that mattered, dressed in clothes that had chosen comfort over declaration. Their face was the kind you might forget until you remembered it perfectly.

“You came,” they said.

“I did,” Mara answered, and then, because it seemed important, “You wrote the letter.”

“I did,” the person agreed. “Though the river had a hand in it.”

Mara considered this. It was not the strangest explanation she had encountered, though it ranked higher than most. “Why me?”

The person smiled, not unkindly. “Because you listen to clocks.”

Mara almost laughed, but the sound caught somewhere between her throat and the air. “That’s not an answer.”

“It’s the only one I have,” they said. “Some people hear time as a series of demands. You hear it as an invitation.”

“And what is it inviting me to?” Mara asked.

The person gestured to the water, to the place where it hesitated. “To notice where you can change by three minutes.”

Mara frowned. “Three minutes doesn’t change anything.”

The person tilted their head, considering. “Are you sure?”

Mara thought of the mornings beneath the clock, the way the second hand moved with such confidence, the way a small shift could ripple outward in ways too subtle to track. She thought of trains missed and caught, of conversations begun too early or too late, of Tuesdays that held together and Tuesdays that did not.

“I don’t know,” she admitted.

“That’s a good place to start,” the person said.

They sat together in a silence that did not ask to be filled. The river continued its soft negotiation with itself, trying on possibilities. A bird traced a line across the sky, then erased it. Somewhere, far away, a clock marked the passing of a minute that would never come again.

“Will I see you again?” Mara asked, after a while.

“Perhaps,” the person said. “But that’s not the point.”

“Then what is?”

They smiled, and this time there was something like mischief in it. “Go home,” they said. “Set your clock three minutes ahead. Or behind. It doesn’t matter which. Then pay attention.”

Mara stood, feeling the weight of the letter in her pocket, the lightness of something else she could not name. “That’s it?”

“That’s it,” the person said.

She turned to leave, then paused. “What happens if I don’t?”

The person looked at the river, at the place where it forgot its name. “Then time will continue to do what it does,” they said. “It will move. You will move with it. There is no punishment in that. Only a missed conversation.”

Mara nodded, as if she understood. Perhaps she did, in the way one understands a melody before learning its notes. She began the walk back, carrying the river’s uncertainty with her like a small, steady flame.

When she reached her apartment, the clock greeted her with its usual impartiality. The hands indicated a time that felt both accurate and negotiable. Mara stood beneath it, considering.

Then, with a hand that was long, pale, and only slightly trembling, she nudged the minute hand forward three minutes.

The clock, as always, kept its counsel. But if it had been the kind of thing that could smile, it might have.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
The vending machine at the end of the corridor had been broken for as long as anyone could remember, which was strange because it was newer than the building itself. It stood there with its fluorescent lights humming softly, rows of perfectly aligned snacks behind glass that never smudged, never cracked, never yielded. The keypad worked, the screen flickered, but nothing ever dropped. People still tried.

Mara tried on Tuesdays.

She wasn’t sure why Tuesdays, exactly. It had started as a coincidence—her first attempt had been on a Tuesday afternoon when she’d found herself lingering in the hallway after a meeting that had run too long and meant too little. She’d pressed B7 for a chocolate bar she didn’t really want, watched the machine blink, pause, and then reset with a polite apology that sounded more like indifference. She had laughed then, a small private laugh, and decided she’d try again the following week.

Months passed. Seasons slipped by unnoticed in the windowless building. The vending machine remained unchanged, and so did Mara’s ritual. Every Tuesday at 3:17 p.m., she would step out of whatever she was doing—email, report, conversation—and walk to the end of the corridor. She would press a button, wait, and leave empty-handed.

It became a kind of anchor.

Other people had theories. Raj from accounting insisted the machine was connected to nothing at all, that it was “just for show,” a prop installed to make the place feel more human. Elise thought it was a psychological experiment, some study in frustration tolerance or delayed gratification. Tom had once kicked it hard enough to bruise his foot and had sworn never to trust anything that looked helpful ever again.

Mara didn’t care what it was. She liked that it didn’t work.

There was something clean about the transaction. You made a choice, you pressed a button, and the universe declined. No complications, no partial successes, no “almost.” Just a clear, quiet no.

On a Tuesday in early autumn, Mara arrived at the machine to find someone else already there.

He was standing a little too close to it, as if proximity might persuade it. He wore a coat despite the building’s steady climate, and his hair was damp, like he had come in from rain that didn’t exist.

“Does it ever work?” he asked without turning around.

“No,” Mara said. “That’s kind of the point.”

He glanced back at her then, eyebrows slightly raised. “The point?”

“For me, at least.” She stepped up beside him. “You press a button. It says no. You move on.”

“That sounds bleak.”

“It’s efficient.”

He considered this, then pressed C3. The machine blinked, hummed, and reset. He let out a soft breath that might have been a laugh. “Efficient disappointment.”

“Exactly.”

He didn’t leave. Instead, he stayed there, hands in his coat pockets, looking at the rows of untouched snacks. “What do you usually get?”

“I don’t.”

“But what do you choose?”

“Varies,” Mara said. “Today I was thinking B2.”

He nodded, as if this were meaningful. “Good choice.”

“Is it?”

“No idea.” He turned back to the machine. “Mind if I try again?”

“It won’t change anything.”

“I know.” He pressed B2.

The machine blinked, hummed, and—

A soft mechanical whir. A shift. A drop.

Both of them stared as a small packet of crackers slid down and landed in the tray with a sound that felt louder than it should have been.

“That’s not—” Mara began, then stopped.

The man reached down slowly, as if the machine might change its mind. He picked up the crackers, turning them over in his hands like an artifact. “Huh.”

“That’s not supposed to happen,” Mara said.

“Clearly, it is,” he replied. But his voice carried the same uncertainty she felt.

She stepped closer, peering into the machine as if she might catch it in the act of being different. Everything looked the same. Perfect rows. Quiet hum. No sign of malfunction or miracle.

“Try again,” he said, offering her space.

Mara hesitated. The ritual had never included success. Success would complicate things.

“Go on,” he said. “B2 again. Or something else.”

She reached out and pressed A5 at random.

The machine blinked.

Paused.

Hummed.

And dropped a bag of chips.

Mara felt something shift in her chest, a small, unsettling tilt. “Okay,” she said slowly. “That’s new.”

“Congratulations,” the man said. “You’ve broken your system.”

“This isn’t my system,” she said. “This is—this is wrong.”

“Wrong in a good way, though.” He gestured at the tray. “You have snacks now.”

“I don’t want them.”

“Then why did you press the button?”

“Because it wasn’t supposed to matter.”

He smiled at that, not unkindly. “It always matters a little.”

Mara picked up the chips, holding them with the same cautious curiosity he had shown. They felt real. Solid. Undeniably present.

“This changes things,” she said.

“It does.”

“I don’t know if I like it.”

He leaned against the wall, watching her. “You could stop coming.”

She shook her head immediately. “No.”

“Then maybe you just… adjust.”

“To what?”

“To the possibility that sometimes it works.”

Mara looked back at the machine. It hummed softly, as it always had, but now the sound seemed different—less like a static background noise and more like something alive, something waiting.

“That’s worse,” she said.

“Why?”

“Because now there’s expectation.”

“And that’s bad?”

“It’s risky.”

He nodded, as if he understood. “Yeah,” he said. “It is.”

They stood there in silence for a moment, the machine between them like a quiet third presence.

“Same time next week?” he asked.

Mara glanced at him. “You’re coming back?”

“Seems like I should,” he said. “For consistency.”

She almost smiled. “Tuesdays at 3:17.”

“I’ll be here.”

He pushed himself off the wall, tucking the crackers into his coat pocket. “Try not to overthink it too much,” he added as he started down the corridor.

“That’s terrible advice,” Mara called after him.

“Probably,” he agreed, without turning around.

When he was gone, Mara looked at the chips in her hand, then back at the machine. She waited a moment, then pressed another button—D6 this time.

The machine blinked.

Paused.

Hummed.

Nothing happened.

Mara exhaled, something like relief settling in her chest. She set the chips on top of the machine and turned to leave, the familiar rhythm restored, if only partially.

As she walked back down the corridor, she realized she was already thinking about next Tuesday—not with certainty, but with a flicker of something new.

Not expectation.

Not quite.

But close.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
The first thing the city forgot was the sound of dogs.

No one noticed at first. The absence slipped in like a skipped heartbeat—subtle, deniable. Someone would pause mid-step and think, wasn’t there usually barking here? Then a bus would hiss, a phone would buzz, and the thought would dissolve. Life edits itself that way, shaving off edges you don’t immediately miss.

By the second week, the silence had shape.

It pooled in alleys where strays used to argue over scraps. It stretched across parks at dusk, where leashes once traced loose constellations between people who never spoke but recognized each other’s rituals. Children asked their parents where the dogs had gone, and the parents said things like, “They’re somewhere safe,” which sounded reassuring until you tried to picture where “somewhere” could be.

Mara noticed because of the mornings.

She woke at 5:52 every day, not by alarm but by the neighbor’s terrier, a stubborn creature that treated sunrise as a personal affront. When the barking stopped, she woke anyway—her body loyal to a signal that no longer existed. The quiet that replaced it wasn’t peaceful. It felt like a room after an argument, still holding the shape of raised voices.

On the fourth morning, she stood at her window and waited, counting seconds the way people do when they want to prove something to themselves. Nothing came. The sky brightened, indifferent.

Across the courtyard, old Mr. Halvorsen stepped out onto his balcony with a cup of tea. He leaned on the railing and listened, brow furrowed. Their eyes met. He lifted his cup slightly, a question disguised as a greeting.

“You hear it?” Mara called.

“Hear what?” he said, then paused, as if catching up to his own answer. “Exactly.”

They stood there, two witnesses to an absence.

The city issued its first statement that afternoon. It appeared on every screen at once, interrupting a cooking video, a lecture on municipal zoning, a livestream of someone assembling a chair they would never sit in. The message was brief: a “temporary displacement of non-human urban fauna” due to “anomalous environmental conditions.” Citizens were advised to remain calm. The tone was so smooth it almost convinced you there was nothing to be worried about, which, of course, meant there was.

Mara worked at the Archive, a squat building that smelled like paper and careful hands. Her job was to catalog the city’s recordings—audio logs, interviews, ambient soundscapes captured over decades. She spent her days labeling the past so it could be found again.

On her lunch break, she queued up a file from three years ago: “Park, 18:07, summer.” The recording opened with wind through leaves, distant laughter, a bicycle bell. Then, layered in, the easy chaos of dogs—panting, tags clinking, a bark that rose like a question and was answered by another across the field. She closed her eyes. The sound was so vivid it seemed to press against her skin.

“Don’t do that to yourself,” said Tomas from the next desk, not looking up. He wore headphones perpetually, a self-imposed border.

“Do what?”

“Go back to before like it’s going to come forward and meet you.”

Mara let the recording play anyway.

That evening, she walked to the park. The grass was trimmed, the paths swept. The city had not abandoned maintenance; it had abandoned explanation. Without dogs, the park felt staged, like a photograph of a place rather than the place itself. People still came. They sat on benches, scrolled through feeds, ate sandwiches that tasted the same as always. But their bodies held a question, a slight lean toward something missing.

Near the old oak, a woman stood holding a leash attached to nothing. She wasn’t looking at it. She was looking ahead, eyes fixed on a horizon that had moved closer without warning.

Mara wanted to ask her something—anything—but language seemed built for presence, not absence. What do you say to someone whose companion has been edited out of the world?

At home, she found a second statement waiting. This one was longer, denser with words that wore lab coats: “temporal discontinuity,” “localized reversion,” “acoustic residue.” The gist, if you peeled away the caution, was this: the city had slipped. Not in space, but in time—by a fraction so small it could not be measured with clocks, only with consequences.

Certain things had not made the transition.

“Why dogs?” Mara said aloud, as if the room might answer.

Her phone chimed. A message from Tomas: come back to the Archive. you need to hear this.

The building at night felt like a held breath. Tomas had dimmed the lights in their section, creating a soft island of focus. On his screen was a waveform, dense and uneven.

“Listen,” he said.

She put on the headphones.

At first, nothing. Then, beneath the expected layers of city noise—a distant engine, the whisper of air systems—there it was. Not a bark, not exactly. More like the outline of one, the ghost of a sound that had forgotten how to fully exist. It flickered at the edge of perception, like a word on the tip of your tongue.

“It’s in all the new recordings,” Tomas said. “Everywhere. Like an echo that doesn’t belong to anything.”

“Acoustic residue,” Mara murmured.

“Or a door,” he said.

They began to map it.

Over the next days, they traced the faint non-sound across the city. It gathered in certain places: the park, of course, but also the bus depot at dawn, the underpass where a woman used to feed a one-eyed mutt, the narrow street behind a bakery where crumbs fell like clockwork at closing time. The residue was stronger where the relationship between humans and dogs had been most routine, most unremarkable. Habit, it seemed, left a deeper groove than novelty.

“Maybe it’s not about dogs,” Tomas said, staring at a map speckled with their markers. “Maybe it’s about repetition. About expectation. The city expects certain things to happen, and when they don’t, it… remembers them.”

“Cities don’t remember,” Mara said.

“Then what’s this?”

She thought of the recordings, the way sound could hold a place even when the place had changed. She thought of her own mornings, her body insisting on waking to a bark that no longer came.

“What if we help it?” she said.

They set up speakers in the park at dusk. It was a small rebellion, easily dismissed as an art installation if anyone asked. They chose recordings carefully—not the loudest or most chaotic, but the ordinary ones. A single bark. The jingle of tags. The soft huff of a dog settling down beside a bench.

People gathered, drawn by curiosity or the promise of something unnamed. The sounds played, tentative at first, then with more confidence, as if remembering themselves in real time. A murmur moved through the crowd, not quite relief, not quite grief.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then, from the edge of the field, a shape.

It wasn’t solid. It wasn’t even fully visible. But it occupied space with intention. It moved like something that knew how to move, that had done it a thousand times without thinking. It paused, head tilted, listening to the sound that had called it.

The woman with the empty leash stepped forward. Her hand trembled, then steadied. She lowered the leash slightly, as if offering it to the air.

“Hey,” she said, voice catching on the simplest word.

The shape approached. The leash lifted, taut against nothing and everything at once.

Around them, the city held its breath, then exhaled.

Mara felt the shift—not in her ears, but in her chest, a pressure easing that she hadn’t known she carried. The sound of a bark—real this time, unmistakable—cut through the evening. It was answered, then answered again, the call and response stitching something back together.

Tomas laughed, a sharp, disbelieving sound. “We didn’t just—”

“We did,” Mara said, though she wasn’t sure what that meant.

In the days that followed, the city relearned its missing pieces. Not all at once, and not perfectly. Some dogs returned as outlines first, then filled in, as if memory needed time to gather substance. Others appeared whole, trotting out of corners of the world that had briefly forgotten them.

The official statements grew quieter, then stopped.

Mara still woke at 5:52, but now the terrier barked again, indignant and alive. The sound felt less like a given and more like a gift, which was not the same thing, but was close enough to carry.

At the Archive, she labeled a new file: “Park, 18:07, after.” She hesitated, then added a note: “Presence restored via imitation. Absence answered with attention.”

She played it back.

Wind, laughter, a bicycle bell.

And beneath it all, steady and certain, the sound the city had almost forgotten—and the echo that would never quite leave, a reminder that what disappears is not always gone, and what returns is never exactly what it was, but something shaped by the act of calling it home.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
The vending machine hummed like it knew something.

Eli noticed it on a Tuesday that felt like it had been photocopied from a hundred other Tuesdays—flat, gray, and slightly misaligned. He had gone down to the basement of the municipal building because the elevator was out again and the stairwell smelled like old rain. The vending machine sat alone in a narrow corridor that led nowhere important, glowing faintly in the fluorescent half-light.

It wasn’t unusual for the building to have a vending machine. What was unusual was that Eli had worked there for six years and had never seen this one before.

It was older than the others, its glass front slightly warped, its buttons mechanical instead of digital. Each slot held items that looked familiar but weren’t quite right. Candy bars with names he almost recognized. Chips in colors that felt off by a shade. Bottles of soda with labels written in fonts that tugged at his memory like a half-remembered dream.

He stepped closer.

There was a small handwritten sign taped to the side:

EXACT CHANGE ONLY  
NO RETURNS

“Figures,” Eli muttered, patting his pockets. He found three quarters and a dime. Not enough for anything, probably.

He was about to leave when the machine made a soft clicking sound. One of the spirals inside twitched, as if adjusting itself. A candy bar shifted forward, its wrapper catching the light.

The name on it was “Midnight Crisp.”

Eli frowned. He didn’t remember that brand, but it sounded… plausible. Like something he might have bought once and forgotten.

He checked his pockets again and, somehow, found two more quarters he was sure hadn’t been there a second ago.

“Okay,” he said quietly. “Weird.”

He fed the coins into the slot. The machine accepted them with a satisfied clunk. He pressed the corresponding button—C7—and watched as the spiral turned, slow and deliberate. The candy bar dropped into the tray with a soft thud.

He picked it up.

The wrapper felt cool, almost cold. He turned it over in his hands, reading the ingredients, though they didn’t quite make sense. Cocoa, sugar, something listed as “nocturne extract.” He laughed under his breath.

“Sure,” he said. “Why not.”

He unwrapped it and took a bite.

The taste was… not chocolate. Not exactly. It was something deeper, darker, like the air just before a storm. It filled his mouth and then, strangely, seemed to expand beyond it, spreading behind his eyes.

And suddenly, he was somewhere else.

He was standing on a street he hadn’t walked in years. The old neighborhood. The one he’d moved away from after his mother died. The streetlights buzzed faintly, and the pavement was cracked in the same places he remembered.

Eli’s breath caught.

“This isn’t—”

But it was. Or it felt like it.

A figure stepped out of the house at the end of the block. A woman, wiping her hands on a dish towel, looking around as if she’d heard something.

His chest tightened.

“Mom?”

She looked up, directly at him, and smiled the way she used to—tired, warm, a little amused.

“You’re late,” she said.

Eli took a step forward, then another. His heart pounded so loudly he could hear it echoing in his ears.

“I—how is this—?”

“You always were slow getting home,” she said, as if he hadn’t spoken. “Dinner’s been ready for half an hour.”

He stopped a few feet away from her. He wanted to hug her, to reach out and make sure she was real, but something held him back. Some quiet instinct that said this was fragile.

“I miss you,” he said instead.

Her expression softened, just a little.

“I know,” she said. “I miss you too.”

The words hung in the air between them, heavier than anything he’d ever carried.

“I didn’t—” He swallowed. “I didn’t get to say goodbye. Not properly.”

She tilted her head, considering him.

“You’re saying it now,” she said.

The streetlight flickered. For a moment, everything dimmed, like a film skipping a frame.

“I have so many things I wish I’d done differently,” Eli said, the words spilling out now. “I should’ve visited more. I should’ve called. I should’ve—”

“Eli.”

Her voice was gentle but firm. The way she used to stop him when he spiraled.

“You were there,” she said. “In the ways that mattered.”

Another flicker. The edges of the world seemed to blur, like paint running in the rain.

“I don’t want this to end,” he said, panic creeping in. “I just got you back.”

She smiled again, but there was something different in it now. Something knowing.

“You didn’t get me back,” she said. “You just found a way to remember me clearly.”

The ground beneath his feet felt less solid.

“Then stay,” he said. “Or let me stay.”

She shook her head.

“You have to go,” she said. “You’ve got your own Tuesdays to deal with.”

Despite himself, Eli let out a small, shaky laugh.

“Yeah,” he said. “I guess I do.”

The world dimmed further, her outline softening.

“Eli?”

“Yeah?”

“Don’t wait for a vending machine to tell people you love them.”

And then she was gone.

—

Eli blinked and found himself back in the basement corridor, the faint hum of the fluorescent lights filling the silence. The vending machine stood in front of him, unchanged, its glass reflecting his slightly dazed expression.

In his hand, the candy bar was half-eaten.

He looked down at it, then back at the machine.

“What the hell,” he whispered.

The machine, as expected, did not answer.

But one of the spirals shifted again, almost imperceptibly.

Eli hesitated, then reached into his pocket. This time, he found exact change without having to look.

He glanced at the rows of unfamiliar items, each one a quiet possibility.

He thought about the street, about his mother’s voice, about all the things he hadn’t said to people still alive.

He exhaled slowly.

“Okay,” he said. “One more.”

He inserted the coins.

The machine hummed, a little louder this time, as if pleased.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
There’s a small town where the clocks don’t agree with one another.

They look ordinary at first. White faces, black hands, polite numerals. But if you stand in the town square and turn in a slow circle, you’ll notice it: the clock above the bakery says 2:14, the one on the post office insists it’s 3:02, and the old iron clock near the fountain claims it’s still morning, stubbornly pointing at 10:17 as if nothing since then has mattered.

No one argues about it.

That’s the strange part. In most places, people would call someone, fix the mechanisms, synchronize everything down to the second. Here, they just shrug and say things like, “Meet me when the bakery clock feels right,” or “I’ll be there after the fountain finishes morning.”

Visitors hate it. They miss trains that haven’t technically arrived yet and show up to dinners that have already ended somewhere else. They complain at first, then grow quiet, then either leave or forget why it bothered them.

I arrived on a Tuesday, or at least that’s what my phone told me. The town sign didn’t mention days, only a painted sentence that had begun to fade:

YOU ARE EXACTLY WHEN YOU NEED TO BE.

I took a room above the bakery. The smell of bread rose through the floorboards in warm, steady waves, more reliable than any clock. The baker, a woman named Elia, handed me a key and said, “Breakfast is whenever you come down hungry.”

“What time do you open?” I asked.

She smiled, as if I’d made a small joke. “When the first loaf is ready.”

That first night, I tried to impose order. I set alarms on my phone, checked the time obsessively, wrote down when I ate, when I slept, when I woke. By morning, the records already disagreed with one another. My phone said I’d slept six hours. My body felt like it had been three days.

Downstairs, Elia slid a plate toward me. “You look like you wrestled a calendar and lost.”

“I just want to know what time it is,” I said.

She leaned on the counter, considering me the way someone might consider a puzzle with no edges. “Why?”

“So I know what I’m supposed to be doing.”

“And what are you supposed to be doing at 9:00 that you couldn’t do at 9:07?”

“That’s not the point.”

“It might be,” she said, and went back to kneading dough.

I spent the afternoon exploring. The town was small but layered, like it had been built several times in the same place and never quite decided which version to keep. Streets bent in ways that didn’t feel accidental. Shops opened and closed without warning. A man painted the same door blue, then green, then blue again, as if testing which version of the day he preferred.

Near the fountain—the one convinced it was still morning—I met an old watchmaker. His shop was cluttered with ticking things: pocket watches, wall clocks, delicate mechanisms laid open like tiny metallic skeletons.

“Can you fix them?” I asked, gesturing toward the window, toward the arguing clocks outside.

“Of course,” he said. “But then they wouldn’t be telling the truth anymore.”

“They’re wrong.”

He shook his head. “They’re honest.”

I must have looked unconvinced, because he picked up a small watch and held it out to me. “What time do you think it is right now?”

I checked my phone. “4:36.”

He nodded. “And how does that feel?”

“Like… late afternoon?”

“Does it feel like you’re almost done with something, or just beginning?”

I hesitated. “Almost done, I guess.”

He smiled. “This watch says 11:12.” He tapped its face. “To it, you’ve barely started.”

“That doesn’t make sense.”

“It doesn’t have to,” he said. “It just has to be useful.”

I left more confused than before, which seemed to be the town’s preferred state for its visitors.

Over the next few days—or what I decided were days—I stopped checking my phone as often. Not out of acceptance, but fatigue. It’s exhausting to argue with an entire place. Instead, I began to follow other cues. Hunger. Light. The sound of the baker pulling trays from the oven. The way conversations in the street swelled and faded like tides.

Something strange happened then: things began to line up.

Not the clocks—they remained defiantly inconsistent—but my sense of things. I arrived places when people were there. I slept when I was tired and woke when I wasn’t. I started a conversation with a woman on a bench just as she was about to leave, and we talked long enough that leaving no longer seemed important.

One evening, or what felt like evening, I returned to the fountain. Its clock still claimed it was morning. The water caught the light in a way that made it impossible to argue with either of them.

The watchmaker was there, sitting on the edge, dangling a hand in the water.

“Have you figured it out?” he asked without looking up.

“I think I stopped trying to,” I said.

“That’s a start.”

I sat beside him. For a while, we said nothing. The town hummed around us—footsteps, distant laughter, a door closing somewhere.

“So what is time here?” I asked finally.

He considered this. “It’s a conversation,” he said. “Not a rule.”

“Between what?”

“Between you and everything else.”

I thought about my first night, the alarms, the lists, the tight grip I’d tried to keep on each minute as if it might escape.

“And if I leave?” I asked.

He shrugged. “The clocks elsewhere will agree with each other again. That doesn’t mean they’re more right. Just louder.”

I stayed longer than I planned. Or shorter. It depends which clock you ask.

When I finally did leave, I stood once more in the town square and turned in a slow circle. The bakery said 2:14. The post office insisted it was 3:02. The fountain held fast to its morning.

For the first time, I didn’t try to choose between them.

I just picked a direction and started walking when it felt like it was time.
:::writing

samples.prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
samples.result:
:::writing
There’s a particular hour just before the city decides what it wants to be for the day. The buses haven’t begun their arguments yet, the storefronts are still thinking about whether to smile, and the pigeons—who act like they own everything—are quietly negotiating with crumbs that don’t yet exist. If you’re awake then, you can almost hear the gears. Not mechanical gears, not really, but something like a soft clicking behind the sky, as if the morning were being assembled out of pale light and half-remembered dreams.

I used to think that hour belonged to insomniacs and bakers. Now I think it belongs to people who have misplaced something they can’t quite name.

On a morning like that, I found a key in my coat pocket. It was an ordinary key in the way that a stone can be ordinary until you realize it has a fossil pressed into it, a fern frozen mid-sigh. Brass, a little worn at the edges, with a head shaped like a teardrop. It had no label, no ribbon, no friendly hint as to what it might unlock. I stood on the corner of Maple and Third, turning it between my fingers, watching how it caught the newborn light.

You’d think I would remember where it came from. I am not a careless person, or at least I like to believe that. I keep my receipts folded in a particular way, my books arranged by the color of their spines, my plants named and spoken to. Yet here was a key with a past I couldn’t access. It felt like a sentence without a verb.

A woman in a red coat passed by and nodded to me as if we shared a secret. For a brief, absurd moment, I considered asking her if she recognized the key, but something about the quiet discouraged interruption. The morning was still deciding.

I started walking without deciding where to go, which is not as strange as it sounds. Some days have gravity, and you simply lean into it. The key warmed in my palm, as if it were aware of its own purpose and impatient with my ignorance. I tried to imagine the lock that would fit it: a small wooden box with a false bottom, a gate leading to a garden no one tended anymore, a drawer in a desk where someone had kept letters tied with twine.

On Elm Street, a bakery exhaled the smell of bread. The baker, flour dusting his eyebrows like a soft snowfall, waved me in. I shook my head, though my stomach made a persuasive argument to the contrary. The key tugged me onward. There’s a particular feeling when you are being led by something you don’t understand; it is both exhilarating and mildly insulting.

I passed the library, which was closed but not asleep. Libraries never sleep; they hum. The key grew warmer. I slowed. The door, heavy and glass-eyed, reflected me back in faint duplication. For a second, I thought I saw a version of myself on the other side, slightly delayed, as if time had hiccupped. I pressed my hand to the door. Locked.

Of course it was.

I took the key and, feeling a little foolish, tried it in the lock. It did not fit. It was too small, too delicate for the library’s old seriousness. I laughed, quiet enough not to disturb the books behind the glass. “All right,” I said to no one, to the key, to the morning. “Not here.”

The city had begun to commit to itself. A bus roared awake. A dog announced its opinions. Somewhere, a radio insisted on a cheerful song that hadn’t yet earned its cheer. I felt the hour slipping, that fragile undecidedness hardening into day. I quickened my pace, as if I could outrun the settling.

There are places that don’t appear on maps, not because they are secret, but because they are shy. They exist in the pauses between streets, in the half-steps people don’t take because they are distracted by their phones or their worries.
